proposals.title: REPLUG-Multi: Retrieval-Augmented Black-Box Adaptation for Diverse Multimodal and Language Tasks
proposals.motivation: The REPLUG framework has demonstrated that augmenting a frozen large language model (LLM) with a trainable retrieval mechanism can significantly improve performance on language modeling and knowledge-intensive benchmarks like MMLU. However, its efficacy on more complex, interactive, and multimodal tasks—such as open-domain QA requiring multi-hop reasoning, dialogue requiring contextual coherence, and vision-language tasks requiring cross-modal grounding—remains unexplored. Evaluating REPLUG's generalization across this broader spectrum is crucial for understanding the limits and potential of retrieval-augmented black-box adaptation as a universal method for enhancing pre-trained models without fine-tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core REPLUG paradigm—training a retriever to select context that maximizes the likelihood of a frozen base model's predictions—can be effectively generalized to diverse downstream tasks beyond factual QA, including open-domain dialogue and multimodal reasoning, by adapting the retrieval objective and data representation to each task's specific structure.
proposals.method: We will extend the original REPLUG (Training LM to Retrieve) framework to three new domains: (1) Open-domain QA (e.g., Natural Questions), using a dense retriever (Contriever or DPR) trained on the LLM's sequence likelihood; (2) Dialogue (e.g., DailyDialog), where the retriever is trained to select context that maximizes the likelihood of the next conversational turn; and (3) Vision-Language tasks (e.g., VQA), integrating the CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) method to create a unified multimodal retrieval corpus of image patches and text, training the retriever on the joint likelihood of a frozen vision-language model (e.g., Flamingo). We will systematically benchmark against task-specific fine-tuning and other retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evaluation of the REPLUG framework's versatility across task modalities, establishing its potential as a general-purpose black-box adaptation method. It will yield new insights into how retrieval objectives must be tailored for different task structures and produce strong, retrieval-augmented baselines for open-domain dialogue and multimodal QA without model parameter updates.
proposals.novelty: While REPLUG has been studied for language modeling, its application to interactive dialogue and, crucially, its extension to multimodal tasks via adaptive image tokenization (CAT) to create a unified retrieval space is novel and aligns with the researcher's expertise in multimodal and reward-based training.

proposals.title: Dual-Use Generation: Synthesizing a Retrieval Corpus with a Vision-Language Model for Enhanced Multimodal QA
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for multimodal tasks is bottlenecked by the availability and coverage of high-quality, task-aligned image-text corpora. Manually curating such corpora is expensive and often fails to cover long-tail or complex compositional queries. This limits the performance and robustness of multimodal systems in knowledge-intensive domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A large vision-language model (VLM), guided by a learned reward model, can generate a synthetic, high-quality corpus of image-text pairs that, when used for retrieval, improves the VLM's own performance on downstream multimodal question-answering tasks compared to using only the original, non-augmented corpus.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained VLM (e.g., based on Flamingo or BLIP-2 architecture) for controlled, corpus-scale generation. Using a two-stage process, we first employ the VLM to generate diverse (image, caption, QA-pair) tuples conditioned on seed knowledge prompts. Second, we filter and rank these synthetic documents using a multimodal reward model (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench) trained to predict downstream QA utility. The top-ranked synthetic documents will be merged with the original retrieval corpus to create a 'Dual-Use' index. We will evaluate retrieval quality and end-to-end QA performance on benchmarks like VQAv2 and ScienceQA, comparing against baselines using the original corpus only.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, scalable method for creating task-adaptive multimodal retrieval corpora. It will provide empirical evidence on the viability and limits of self-improving retrieval systems via generation. The resulting framework and analysis will inform future work on closing the loop between generation and retrieval in multimodal contexts.
proposals.novelty: While text-based LMs have been used to generate retrieval corpora, applying this 'dual-use' principle to the multimodal domain—where both image and text generation must be coherent and useful—is unexplored. The key innovation is the tight integration of a multimodal reward model for quality control, moving beyond simple likelihood-based filtering.

proposals.title: Unified Reasoning via Joint Graph Ensembles of Language Models and Knowledge Graphs
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal systems often rely on a single large model or simple ensemble methods, which struggle with complex reasoning requiring diverse knowledge sources and reasoning styles. Fusing the complementary strengths of multiple specialized Language Models (LMs) and structured Knowledge Graphs (KGs) remains an open challenge, limiting robustness and performance on tasks like visual question answering and knowledge-intensive generation.
proposals.hypothesis: Constructing a joint, dynamic reasoning graph that explicitly connects the latent representations and inference paths of multiple LMs and KGs will yield more accurate, reliable, and interpretable multimodal reasoning than monolithic models or late-fusion ensembles.
proposals.method: We will develop a graph neural architecture where nodes represent intermediate states (e.g., token embeddings, KG entity embeddings) from different models, and edges represent learned, attention-based compatibility and transition functions. Inspired by CAT's adaptive tokenization, we will design content-adaptive graph sparsification to manage complexity. Training will use a multi-objective reward signal, informed by insights from Multimodal RewardBench, to jointly optimize for task performance and internal consistency across the ensemble, mitigating over-reliance on any single flawed source.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for scalable model fusion, a benchmark for evaluating ensemble reasoning consistency, and new insights into how heterogeneous models can be jointly trained for complementary reasoning. It advances beyond simple output aggregation towards integrated, graph-structured inference.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior ensemble or retrieval-augmented methods, this approach jointly reasons *through* a dynamically constructed graph of internal model states, enabling fine-grained interaction and validation between disparate knowledge sources and reasoning modules during the inference process itself.

proposals.title: Content-Adaptive Self-Training for Robust Vision-Language Models with Minimal Source Supervision
proposals.motivation: Modern vision-language models (VLMs) require vast, labeled datasets from the source distribution for robust performance, which is costly and often infeasible for specialized domains. This reliance creates a bottleneck for deploying robust models in real-world scenarios where labeled source data is scarce. Pre-2026, methods for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection and robustness often presuppose abundant source data, failing to address this fundamental data-efficiency challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A VLM can achieve robustness to distribution shifts by leveraging its own generative and discriminative capabilities for self-supervised adaptation, minimizing dependence on large-scale labeled source data. We hypothesize that content-adaptive tokenization can guide this process to focus learning on semantically relevant features.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we adapt the CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) method to generate multi-granular visual tokens that emphasize semantically stable regions without source labels. Second, we employ a self-training loop where the model's own generations (via a frozen LLM backbone) create pseudo-labels for unlabeled target data, with a multimodal reward model—inspired by RewardBench principles—acting as a learned filter to select high-quality pseudo-labels and avoid confirmation bias. Training will use a contrastive loss between adapted visual tokens and filtered textual pseudo-labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for training robust VLMs with minimal labeled source data, directly challenging a core assumption in the field. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating robustness under source-data scarcity and demonstrate the synergistic potential of content-adaptive tokenization with self-supervised reward-guided learning.
proposals.novelty: The integration of content-adaptive tokenization (CAT) with a reward-model-guided self-training loop specifically for the low-source-data regime is novel. Unlike prior self-training or OOD works, our method systematically uses internal model confidence and multimodal reward signals to replace, not just augment, the need for source labels.

proposals.title: Streaming RewardBench: A Framework for Continuous Evaluation and Adaptation of Vision-Language Reward Models
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal reward model (RM) benchmarks, like Multimodal RewardBench, provide static snapshots of performance, which is insufficient for real-world deployment. In practice, VLMs encounter streaming data with non-stationary distributions, leading to reward model degradation and misalignment over time. A framework for continuous evaluation and adaptation is critical for maintaining safe and effective VLM alignment in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a streaming benchmark with online evaluation protocols and content-adaptive retraining will enable reward models to maintain robustness and improve generalization under continuous distribution shifts.
proposals.method: We will extend Multimodal RewardBench into a streaming framework where data points (image-text pairs with human preferences) arrive sequentially. We will implement online evaluation metrics (e.g., moving-window accuracy, drift detection) and integrate CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) for efficient, on-the-fly feature extraction. The core adaptation will use online learning algorithms, such as a rehearsal-based buffer system combined with experience replay, to selectively update the reward model on detected shifts while mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first benchmark and methodology for continuously evaluating multimodal reward models on streaming data. It will yield insights into failure modes of RMs under drift and establish best practices for online alignment, directly impacting the long-term reliability of deployed VLMs.
proposals.novelty: While online learning exists, its application to multimodal reward modeling for VLMs is unexplored. The novelty lies in fusing a dynamic benchmark with content-adaptive tokenization and online adaptation specifically for the reward learning paradigm.

proposals.title: Cross-Attention for Cross-Modal Alignment: Adapting Vision-Language Mechanisms for Text-Graph Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Aligning unstructured text with structured graph data (e.g., knowledge graphs, molecular graphs) is a core challenge for multimodal reasoning and knowledge-intensive tasks. Pre-2026 methods often rely on simple projection layers or separate encoders, failing to capture fine-grained, compositional relationships between textual concepts and graph entities. Adapting the powerful cross-attention mechanisms proven in vision-language models (VLMs) offers a principled path to learn these alignments more effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: Cross-attention layers from pre-trained VLMs, when structurally adapted and fine-tuned, can serve as superior alignment modules for text-graph tasks compared to standard projection-based methods, by enabling dynamic, context-aware fusion of textual and graph-structured features.
proposals.method: We will extract and adapt cross-attention modules from a pre-trained VLM (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2). The graph (node/edge features) will be encoded via a Graph Neural Network (GNN) into a sequence of token embeddings. These graph tokens and text tokens will be fed into the adapted cross-attention block, allowing bidirectional, content-adaptive feature fusion. We will train this model using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) for alignment, followed by task-specific fine-tuning on downstream benchmarks like knowledge-graph QA or molecular property prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, general-purpose architecture for text-graph alignment, demonstrated through improved performance on structured reasoning benchmarks. It will also yield insights into the transferability of cross-modal attention mechanisms across different structural modalities (images vs. graphs).
proposals.novelty: While cross-attention is standard in VLMs, its direct adaptation and systematic evaluation for aligning sequential text with non-grid, relational graph data is unexplored. This moves beyond using VLMs for images to repurpose their core alignment mechanism for a fundamentally different structural modality.

proposals.title: Content-Adaptive Few-Shot Tuning: Leveraging Multimodal Reward Models for Efficient Vision-Language Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are data-hungry and expensive to fine-tune for new tasks, limiting their practical deployment. While few-shot and zero-shot learning offer a solution, current methods often underperform due to suboptimal adaptation signals and inefficient tokenization for novel visual concepts. This research addresses the critical need for sample-efficient adaptation in multimodal AI, a key challenge pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a content-adaptive image tokenizer, guided by a holistic multimodal reward model, can generate task-specific, data-efficient adaptation signals that enable VLMs to achieve strong performance with minimal examples by focusing learning on the most semantically relevant visual features.
proposals.method: We will build upon the researcher's CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) framework to create a dynamic tokenization strategy that prioritizes image regions deemed important by a reward model. We will leverage insights from Multimodal RewardBench to train or select a robust reward model that evaluates multimodal alignment. This reward signal will be used to weight the visual tokens during few-shot fine-tuning, creating a focused, loss-weighted training objective. The approach will be tested against standard few-shot fine-tuning and prompt-tuning baselines on established VLM benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, parameter-efficient fine-tuning algorithm for VLMs that significantly reduces the required number of training examples. It will provide new insights into the interplay between tokenization efficiency, reward modeling, and few-shot generalization, advancing the field of sample-efficient multimodal learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of a content-adaptive tokenizer with a holistic multimodal reward model specifically for the purpose of few-shot learning, moving beyond using these components in isolation for generation or evaluation. This creates a targeted adaptation mechanism not present in prior work.

proposals.title: Dialogue Domain Shift: A Low-Resource, Few-Shot Benchmark for Evaluating Multimodal Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models, especially Vision-Language Models (VLMs), are often evaluated on static benchmarks, failing to measure their ability to adapt to new, specialized domains with limited data—a critical requirement for real-world deployment. Pre-2026, benchmarks like RewardBench assess general alignment but not rapid adaptation, and methods like CAT for adaptive tokenization lack a standardized test for few-shot, cross-modal dialogue learning. This gap leaves model adaptability under-measured.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current VLMs and their training paradigms, including reward modeling and content-adaptive tokenization, are insufficient for robust few-shot adaptation to novel dialogue domains, and that a structured benchmark will reveal specific failure modes in knowledge integration and reward generalization under low-resource constraints.
proposals.method: We will design a benchmark comprising multiple novel dialogue domains (e.g., technical troubleshooting with diagrams, specialized cultural storytelling) each defined by only 3-5 example dialogues (image-text pairs). We will evaluate adaptation along two axes: 1) **Generation Performance**: using few-shot in-context learning and parameter-efficient fine-tuning (e.g., LoRA) of base VLMs, and 2) **Reward Generalization**: testing if pre-trained multimodal reward models can accurately score responses in the new domain. The evaluation will integrate out-of-distribution detection metrics to analyze adaptation confidence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first focused benchmark for low-resource, few-shot multimodal dialogue adaptation, enabling systematic evaluation of model agility. It will yield insights into how adaptive tokenization, reward modeling, and training strategies affect cross-domain generalization, guiding future research toward more data-efficient multimodal systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing multimodal benchmarks focused on broad performance, this proposal uniquely combines few-shot dialogue, domain shift, and holistic evaluation of both generation and reward models—directly extending the researcher's work on CAT and Multimodal RewardBench into a novel, challenging adaptation setting.

proposals.title: HyperLoss: A Meta-Learning Framework for Dynamic, Compositional Loss Function Assembly in Multi-Modal Models
proposals.motivation: Current multi-modal learning relies on fixed, hand-designed loss functions (e.g., cross-entropy, contrastive, reconstruction) that are often suboptimal and require extensive tuning for each new task-modality combination. This limits model adaptability and generalization, especially for complex objectives like reward modeling and out-of-distribution detection. A unified, learnable framework for constructing loss functions could streamline training and improve performance across diverse vision-language tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A hypernetwork that dynamically assembles a composite loss from a small set of interpretable, statistical primitives (e.g., distribution matching, consistency, sparsity) conditioned on task and modality descriptors will yield more effective and transferable training objectives than static, monolithic losses for multi-modal models.
proposals.method: We will define a library of foundational loss primitives, each implementing a core statistical operation. A conditioning hypernetwork, taking as input a concatenated task embedding and modality descriptor vector (e.g., from CAT tokenizer statistics), will output weights and mixing parameters for these primitives. This composite loss will be used to train a downstream multi-modal model (e.g., a VLM). The hypernetwork and the main model will be co-trained end-to-end, with the hypernetwork's parameters updated via gradient descent through the loss computation graph, enabling meta-learning of the loss function itself.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a flexible, unified framework for loss function design, reducing the need for manual objective engineering. It will offer new insights into which loss characteristics are most beneficial for specific data modalities and tasks, potentially improving performance on benchmarks like RewardBench and robustness in OOD settings. The framework itself will be a novel, reusable component for multi-modal learning systems.
proposals.novelty: While hypernetworks have been used for weight generation, their application to dynamically assemble interpretable loss functions from primitives conditioned on modality/task context is novel. This moves beyond tuning loss weights to learning the loss structure itself, creating a meta-objective that adapts to the data's statistical properties.

proposals.title: Unifying Generative Objectives: A Thermodynamic Free Energy Framework for Multimodal Models
proposals.motivation: Current generative AI relies on distinct, modality-specific training objectives (e.g., cross-entropy for language, variational/score matching for images), creating a fragmented landscape. This complicates the development of truly unified multimodal models that can seamlessly generate and reason across discrete and continuous data types. A common theoretical framework is needed to understand, compare, and interpolate between these training paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: A generalized objective, analogous to thermodynamic free energy, can unify discrete and continuous generative modeling by balancing a data fidelity term and an entropy term, recovering standard losses as special cases and enabling new hybrid training regimes.
proposals.method: We will formalize a 'Machine Learning Free Energy' (MLFE) objective as L = E_{x~p_data}[D(p_model(·|x) || p_target(·|x))] - β * H(p_model), where D is a chosen divergence, H is entropy, and β is a temperature-like parameter. We will instantiate this framework in transformer-based architectures, using the CAT tokenizer for adaptive image representation. By varying β and D (e.g., KL divergence, Wasserstein distance), we will analytically and empirically demonstrate how the objective reduces to cross-entropy (β=1, discrete) or connects to diffusion ELBOs (β as noise scale, continuous). Training will be evaluated on multimodal benchmarks, measuring performance and generation diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles theoretical lens connecting disparate generative modeling approaches. It will yield a practical, tunable objective for training hybrid models that balance fidelity and diversity, directly applicable to next-generation VLMs. The framework will also offer new tools for analyzing model behavior and calibration through the thermodynamic analogy.
proposals.novelty: While free energy concepts are used in variational inference and reinforcement learning, applying them as a unifying *generative training objective* to bridge discrete and continuous data modalities is novel. This moves beyond using free energy as a mere analysis tool to actively designing a single, parameterized loss function for multimodal generation.

proposals.title: Architectural Fingerprinting: Disentangling Data Characteristics via Non-Transformer Backbone Probing
proposals.motivation: Current model auditing and safety techniques often treat large models as black boxes, making it difficult to attribute properties like memorization or bias to specific design choices versus the underlying training data. With the rise of alternative architectures like Mamba (state-space models) and the integration of multimodal training objectives, a critical opportunity exists to use architectural diversity as a lens for data forensics. This research addresses the urgent need for more transparent model analysis, moving beyond transformer-centric assumptions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that probing models built on non-transformer backbones (e.g., state-space models, RWKV) trained on combined next-token and diffusion objectives will yield data fingerprints that are less entangled with transformer-specific inductive biases, allowing for a clearer distillation of fundamental training data characteristics.
proposals.method: We will train a suite of models (transformers, Mamba, RWKV) on identical multimodal datasets (e.g., LAION) using a unified objective combining autoregressive language modeling and diffusion-based image generation. We will then apply and adapt probing methods from prior work (e.g., canonical correlation analysis, linear classifier probes) to intermediate representations to extract 'architectural fingerprints.' These fingerprints, which encode statistical properties of the training data, will be systematically compared across architectures using metrics from the OOD detection and model attribution literature to quantify their specificity and generality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for architectural-aware data distillation, providing a more nuanced framework for model auditing. It will produce concrete findings on which data characteristics are architecture-invariant versus transformer-specific, directly informing safer and more transparent model design practices.
proposals.novelty: While data fingerprinting and model probing exist, systematically using non-transformer backbones as a controlled variable to disentangle architectural bias from core data characteristics is a novel approach. This moves beyond simply benchmarking performance to using architectural diversity as a diagnostic tool for model transparency.

proposals.title: UniTrans: Learning Universal Sensory Transducers for Homogeneous Multimodal Processing
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models rely on distinct, handcrafted encoders for each modality (e.g., ViTs, audio spectrogram CNNs), creating architectural silos that hinder the discovery of truly unified representations. This fragmentation complicates cross-modal alignment and limits efficiency. Inspired by biological sensory systems, which transduce diverse inputs into a common neural code, we propose a radical simplification: replacing fixed encoders with learnable, lightweight transducers that project all modalities into a single, shared token space for processing by a homogeneous core.
proposals.hypothesis: A single transformer core, fed by minimal, learnable modality-specific transduction layers trained end-to-end, can discover a more unified and efficient computational substrate for vision, language, and audio than a system of specialized encoders, leading to improved cross-modal alignment and data efficiency.
proposals.method: We will design small, convolutional or linear 'transducer' modules that take near-raw inputs (image patches, audio waveforms, byte-level text) and project them into a d-dimensional latent space. These transducers, one per modality, will feed into a single, standard transformer backbone. All parameters will be trained jointly via a multi-task objective combining masked token modeling (in the unified latent space) and cross-modal contrastive alignment (e.g., InfoNCE loss). We will benchmark against established encoders on tasks from Multimodal RewardBench and out-of-distribution generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete architecture and training framework for universal sensory transduction, challenging the necessity of deep, modality-specific encoders. It will yield empirical evidence on the feasibility and benefits of a homogeneous processing pathway, potentially offering a more parameter-efficient and aligned foundation for next-generation multimodal systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work explores joint training or shared backbones, the proposal to replace deep, established encoders with minimal, learnable transducers trained from scratch for a homogeneous core is a significant architectural departure. It directly tests the necessity of inductive biases baked into current encoders versus those discoverable through end-to-end learning.

proposals.title: Unified Scaling Laws: Emergent Cross-Modal Reasoning in Homogeneous Multimodal Transformers
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art multimodal models (e.g., Flamingo, GATO) rely on modality-specific encoders and complex fusion modules, creating architectural heterogeneity that may hinder efficient cross-modal transfer and complicate scaling analyses. A truly homogeneous architecture, where all modalities share a single projection and transformer backbone, offers a cleaner testbed for studying the fundamental scaling behavior of unified intelligence. Establishing scaling laws for this simplified paradigm is crucial for predicting performance and allocating compute efficiently in future multimodal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a homogeneous multimodal transformer, forced to share parameters across all modalities, will exhibit accelerated emergence of unified representations and superior cross-modal reasoning abilities at scale, leading to more favorable compute-optimal scaling laws compared to heterogeneous architectures.
proposals.method: We will design a series of models from 1B to 100B+ parameters using a single linear projection layer to map tokenized text, images (using a method like CAT for adaptive tokenization), audio, and video into a shared embedding space, followed by a standard decoder-only transformer stack. We will train these models on a massive, temporally aligned dataset spanning all modalities (e.g., a scaled-up version of WebVid-10M with paired audio and text). We will fit scaling laws to model performance on a comprehensive evaluation suite (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench) measuring cross-modal retrieval, generation, and reasoning, analyzing how metrics scale with compute, data, and parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for homogeneous multimodal architectures, providing a predictive framework for their development. It will deliver a rigorous comparison of representation quality and efficiency between homogeneous and heterogeneous paradigms, informing future architectural choices. The released models and analysis will serve as a foundational benchmark for unified multimodal learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for unimodal language models, deriving them for a forced-parameter-share, all-modality homogeneous transformer is novel. This directly tests the hypothesis that architectural simplification, not complexity, is key to efficient multimodal intelligence, moving beyond the established trend of modality-specific components.

proposals.title: Homogeneous Multimodal Modeling: A Single-Stack Transformer for Vision, Language, and Reward
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models (e.g., VLMs) rely on modality-specific encoders (e.g., separate image backbones and text tokenizers) and fusion layers, creating architectural complexity and potential information bottlenecks. This heterogeneity complicates training, limits cross-modal knowledge transfer, and hinders the development of truly unified representations. A homogeneous architecture could simplify scaling, improve efficiency, and test the fundamental limits of a single, shared representational space.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer model trained from scratch with a single, shared embedding space and a unified processing stack for all input modalities can achieve competitive performance on multimodal tasks without sacrificing the ability to perform strong unimodal reasoning.
proposals.method: We will develop a model based on a decoder-only transformer. Inspired by CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization), we will adaptively tokenize images into a sequence of discrete tokens using a pre-trained, frozen VQ-VAE, treating them as a 'foreign language.' Text will be tokenized via a standard subword tokenizer. All tokens will be projected via a single, shared embedding matrix into a common space. The model will be trained from scratch using a next-token prediction objective on a large, interleaved corpus of image-text sequences and pure text. We will integrate reward modeling, drawing on RewardBench, by training the homogeneous model as a reward model on preference data to assess its alignment capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the feasibility and limits of fully homogeneous multimodal architectures. It will deliver a simplified model blueprint that reduces engineering overhead and could improve cross-modal generalization. The integration of reward modeling will also offer new insights into training aligned agents within a unified representational framework.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like FLAVA or unified tokenizers exist, training a decoder-only transformer from scratch with a single embedding and transformer stack for both vision and language—without any modality-specific encoding layers—remains largely unexplored. This approach pushes beyond adapter-based fusion or late-interaction models toward a more radical architectural unification.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Integration as Primary Objective: A Shared Latent Space Optimized for Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models, such as CLIP or Flamingo, often align modalities via contrastive or generative losses that treat cross-modal tasks as secondary to strong unimodal representations. This can lead to suboptimal performance on integrative tasks like visual question answering or cross-modal retrieval, where the binding of concepts across modalities is paramount. Drawing inspiration from cognitive neuroscience, which shows the brain prioritizes integrated percepts, we hypothesize that inverting this design priority is key to more robust multimodal understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: A shared latent space explicitly optimized by a primary cross-modal reconstruction loss (e.g., image-from-text) will yield superior performance on complex integrative tasks, even if it necessitates a controlled decrease in standalone unimodal benchmark performance, as the architecture becomes fundamentally wired for cross-modal binding.
proposals.method: We will design an encoder-decoder architecture where a shared latent bottleneck is trained primarily with a cross-modal reconstruction objective (e.g., a diffusion-based decoder generating images from text latents). Uni-modal encoders (e.g., ViT, LLM) will project into this space and be regularized by it, rather than being trained independently. We will adapt techniques from the researcher's CAT work for adaptive tokenization to manage information flow. Training will use a composite loss heavily weighted toward cross-modal reconstruction, with auxiliary losses for unimodal autoencoding acting as regularizers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural paradigm for multimodal learning that prioritizes integration over isolation. It will yield a new model, benchmarked against existing VLMs on integrative tasks from Multimodal RewardBench, and offer insights into the trade-offs between unimodal fidelity and cross-modal capability.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard design goal: making cross-modal reconstruction the primary objective and unimodal encoding an auxiliary, regularized component. This is a deliberate departure from methods that first perfect unimodal representations and then align them, proposing a biologically-inspired, integration-first approach.

proposals.title: TokenAudit: Diagnosing and Correcting Inherited Defects in Discrete-Token Visual Generative Models
proposals.motivation: Large-scale visual generative models, such as diffusion models, often inherit and propagate systemic defects—including social biases, safety violations, and structural artifacts—from their training data. These homogenized failures are difficult to audit and correct in continuous latent spaces. The rise of discrete visual tokenization (e.g., VQ-VAE, VQ-GAN) offers a new opportunity, as it provides a structured, language-model-like representation for images, but methods to systematically audit and repair defects in this token space are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Inherited visual model defects manifest as identifiable, skewed distributions of discrete tokens, and targeted interventions on these token distributions can correct the defects at their source without degrading overall model performance.
proposals.method: We will first train a high-fidelity, content-adaptive image tokenizer (extending CAT) to create a discrete codebook. We will then audit pre-trained generative models (e.g., autoregressive transformers over these tokens) by analyzing token co-occurrence statistics and attention patterns to pinpoint distributions correlated with known defects (e.g., biased attributes). For repair, we will develop and compare two techniques: 1) a reward-guided fine-tuning method, using a multimodal reward model (inspired by RewardBench) to shift token probabilities, and 2) a constrained decoding algorithm that masks or re-weights problematic token candidates during generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel auditing framework for discrete-token visual models and a suite of targeted repair techniques. It will provide empirical evidence on the tractability of fixing inherited defects in token space, contributing to safer and more controllable multimodal generation.
proposals.novelty: While discrete tokenization for images is established, its systematic use as an *auditable and repairable* medium for correcting inherited model flaws is novel. This moves beyond post-hoc filtering or classifier guidance to enable direct, interpretable intervention in the generative process.

proposals.title: Discrete Visual Tokens: A Unified Language-Image Modeling Framework for Coherent Multimodal Generation and Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art image generation relies heavily on continuous diffusion models, which are architecturally and procedurally distinct from the discrete, autoregressive frameworks that dominate language modeling. This divergence creates a significant barrier to building truly unified multimodal models capable of seamless joint reasoning and generation across text and visual modalities. A discrete, token-based approach could enable more efficient training, better integration of world knowledge, and more coherent cross-modal tasks, but existing methods often treat discretization as a secondary step rather than a foundational design principle.
proposals.hypothesis: By enforcing a discrete, high-information-density latent representation for images from the outset, we can train a single, unified autoregressive transformer model that achieves competitive image generation fidelity while enabling superior multimodal reasoning and controllability compared to hybrid continuous-discrete systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel two-stage framework. First, we will train a content-adaptive image tokenizer (extending concepts from CAT) to map images to a discrete codebook of visual tokens, prioritizing semantic fidelity and compact sequence length. Second, we will train a decoder-only transformer on a large-scale interleaved corpus of text and these visual tokens, treating both modalities as a single stream of discrete symbols. We will incorporate a multimodal reward model (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench) during fine-tuning to align model outputs with human preferences for coherence and quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete architecture and training recipe for a fully discrete, unified language-image model, challenging the dominance of diffusion-based pipelines. We will contribute rigorous benchmarks demonstrating its capabilities in both generative and discriminative tasks, and release a new diagnostic suite for evaluating the joint reasoning abilities of multimodal token-based models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses discrete tokens for compression or as an input to diffusion models, our proposal is novel in its core insistence on a purely discrete, autoregressive modeling paradigm for both modalities from the start, eliminating continuous latent spaces entirely to create a more homogeneous and potentially more scalable multimodal foundation model.

proposals.title: TransFusion-X: A Unified Multimodal Framework via a Universal Signal Encoder
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models like Transfusion primarily unify text and images, but real-world data includes audio, video, and 3D signals. Processing these modalities with separate, siloed encoders creates architectural complexity and hinders genuine cross-modal understanding and generation. Drawing inspiration from sensor fusion in robotics, which maps heterogeneous inputs to a common representation like an occupancy grid, we propose a method to achieve true modality-agnostic processing for foundation models.
proposals.hypothesis: A trainable Universal Signal Encoder, projecting diverse raw signals (audio spectrograms, video frames, 3D point clouds) into the same latent space as text and image tokens from Transfusion, will enable effective co-training and emergent cross-modal capabilities without sacrificing performance on core modalities.
proposals.method: We will design a modular encoder with modality-specific front-ends (e.g., ConvNets for spectrograms, 3D sparse CNNs for point clouds) that feed into a shared transformer backbone, outputting token sequences compatible with Transfusion's latent space. This encoder will be integrated into the Transfusion framework and co-trained using a combination of contrastive alignment losses (inspired by CLIP) and masked token modeling objectives across all modalities. Training will leverage existing paired datasets (e.g., AudioSet, HowTo100M, ShapeNet) and the model's performance will be benchmarked on retrieval, conditional generation, and zero-shot reasoning tasks from Multimodal RewardBench.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the Transfusion recipe into a truly general-purpose multimodal foundation model capable of processing five core modalities. It will provide a scalable architectural blueprint for modality expansion and establish new benchmarks for holistic evaluation of unified models, moving beyond text-image pairs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses separate encoders for each modality, our approach introduces a *single* parameter-shared encoder for all non-textual signals, explicitly inspired by the sensor fusion principle of a unified intermediate representation. This differs from simply adding new encoders in a mixture-of-experts style.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Data Transparency: Auditing Ultra-Large Transfusion Models via Inferred Training Data Properties
proposals.motivation: As vision-language models (VLMs) scale to hundreds of billions of parameters, their training datasets become opaque, raising critical concerns about data provenance, copyright, and bias. Existing data inference techniques are studied on smaller models, leaving a fundamental gap: we do not know how data extractability and the nature of the inferred properties change with extreme model scale. This work addresses the urgent need for scalable, principled auditing tools for frontier multimodal models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness and granularity of training data inference follow predictable scaling laws with model size, and that ultra-large Transfusion models exhibit emergent, quantifiable signatures of data composition and memorization not present in smaller variants.
proposals.method: We will pretrain a controlled series of Transfusion (encoder-decoder) models from 7B to ~300B parameters on a fixed, documented multimodal corpus. We will systematically apply and extend state-of-the-art inference methods, including gradient-based attribution adapted from our CAT tokenizer for efficient feature analysis, and membership inference attacks. Crucially, we will measure inference performance (e.g., precision/recall for data property recovery) as a function of model scale, compute, and training data order, fitting scaling laws to these metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first scaling laws for data transparency, providing a predictive framework for auditing frontier models. It will deliver a novel methodology and benchmark for scaling-aware data inference, enabling more rigorous characterization of data footprints, biases, and memorization risks in massive VLMs.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for performance, no work has established them for data *extractability*. The two-way study—using scale to understand inference and inference to understand scale—creates a novel synergy for model auditing beyond isolated scale or data studies.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Cross-Modal Integration in Vision-Language Models: A Process Dissociation Framework
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for vision-language models (VLMs) primarily measure aggregate task accuracy, which conflates different underlying reasoning strategies. This makes it difficult to discern whether a model is performing genuine cross-modal integration or relying on superficial, modality-biased heuristics. A more diagnostic evaluation is needed to guide the development of robust, interpretable multimodal AI.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the Process Dissociation Procedure from cognitive psychology, we can design benchmark tasks that quantitatively decompose a VLM's performance into estimates of its automatic, modality-specific processing versus its controlled, integrative cross-modal reasoning.
proposals.method: We will construct a diagnostic benchmark suite with paired 'Inclusion' and 'Exclusion' tasks. For a given concept, an Inclusion task requires fusing complementary information from image and text (e.g., answer using both). The corresponding Exclusion task requires using information from one modality (e.g., the image) while actively ignoring contradictory or misleading information planted in the other (e.g., the caption). We will derive formal equations from the PDP framework to estimate the contributions of automatic (modality-specific) and controlled (integrative) processes from the performance patterns across these conditions. This benchmark will be applied to analyze state-of-the-art VLMs (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2) and training variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a novel diagnostic toolkit that moves beyond accuracy to reveal the *how* behind model performance. It will produce new, interpretable metrics for cross-modal integration and identify failure modes in current VLM training paradigms, such as over-reliance on a single modality.
proposals.novelty: While existing work (e.g., Multimodal RewardBench) evaluates model outputs, this proposal innovates by adapting a well-established *cognitive science paradigm* to create a controlled experimental framework that isolates specific internal processing components within AI models.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Reasoning Benchmarks (CMRB): A Diagnostic Framework for Unified Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for unified vision-language models (VLMs) like Transfusion often rely on end-task performance (e.g., VQA accuracy) or simple retrieval, which conflate pattern recognition with genuine cross-modal reasoning. This makes it difficult to diagnose specific weaknesses in compositional understanding, causal reasoning, or fine-grained attribute binding across modalities. A more diagnostic benchmark is needed to guide the development of models with robust, human-like multimodal understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a benchmark with controlled, procedurally generated tasks that isolate specific reasoning skills (e.g., negation, spatial relation composition, counterfactual reasoning) will reveal significant, systematic failures in state-of-the-art unified VLMs that are obscured by current holistic evaluations.
proposals.method: We will develop Cross-Modal Reasoning Benchmarks (CMRB) as a suite of diagnostic tasks. Inspired by CAT's adaptive tokenization, we will generate synthetic image-text pairs where specific reasoning dimensions (logic, quantification, spatial relations) are systematically varied while controlling for visual and linguistic complexity. We will design tasks requiring models to generate or select text based on fine-grained image reasoning, and vice-versa. Evaluation will move beyond accuracy to include consistency under perturbation and analysis of failure modes, leveraging insights from OOD detection research to distinguish reasoning failures from distributional shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a fine-grained, diagnostic benchmark for evaluating cross-modal reasoning, moving beyond monolithic scores. It will produce a detailed failure mode analysis for current unified models like Transfusion, identifying precise architectural or training limitations. The benchmark's design principles will inform future model development and evaluation toward more robust multimodal AI.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing benchmarks (e.g., VQAv2, GQA), CMRB explicitly decouples and isolates specific reasoning skills in a controlled synthetic environment, enabling causal attribution of model weaknesses. It integrates procedural generation with diagnostic evaluation frameworks, a novel combination for multimodal assessment.

proposals.title: Data Assimilation for Training: An Adaptive Curriculum for Multi-Modal Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Training large multi-modal models on massive, heterogeneous datasets is computationally prohibitive. Current methods treat all data tokens uniformly, leading to inefficient learning from redundant or uninformative sequences. Drawing inspiration from Numerical Weather Prediction, where data assimilation optimally integrates sparse observations into a physical forecast model, we propose a novel paradigm to make multi-modal training far more sample-efficient.
proposals.hypothesis: A training framework that dynamically assimilates multi-modal data tokens based on real-time estimates of their informativeness and the model's current learning state will accelerate convergence and improve final model performance compared to standard uniform sampling.
proposals.method: We will develop a meta-controller that maintains a state representation of the model's learning progress ('forecast'). For each incoming batch of mixed-modality tokens (e.g., from text, image patches via CAT, or audio), the controller will compute an assimilation weight using a learned function. This function will ingest metrics of batch uncertainty, novelty relative to recent training history, and alignment with current model performance gaps (e.g., from a reward model or validation loss). The core training will use a transformer-based architecture, but the data scheduler will prioritize high-weight batches for gradient updates, effectively creating a dynamic, uncertainty-aware curriculum.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled framework for adaptive data scheduling in large-scale training, directly reducing computational cost. We will release a benchmark for evaluating training efficiency on mixed-modality streams and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on multimodal tasks from VLMs to audio-language models.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning exists, applying the formal, uncertainty-quantifying framework of data assimilation from NWP to token-level online scheduling in multi-modal training is a novel cross-disciplinary analogy. It moves beyond static heuristics to a closed-loop, forecast-aware system.

proposals.title: Coordinated Encoders with Shared Alignment (CoESA): A Unified Framework for Fidelity-Preserving Multimodal Latent Spaces
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models often treat modality-specific encoding and cross-modal alignment as separate or sequential problems, leading to a trade-off where improvements in single-modality representation (e.g., via advanced tokenization like CAT) can degrade cross-modal understanding and reward modeling. This misalignment is a core challenge for robust VLM performance and reliable out-of-distribution generalization, as highlighted by pre-2026 work on RewardBench and OOD detection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly coordinating the training of sophisticated, modality-specific encoders through a small, shared alignment network and a central coordination module will create a unified latent space that simultaneously maximizes single-modality fidelity and cross-modal semantic consistency, leading to superior performance on both generation and understanding tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop modality-specific encoders (e.g., a vision encoder building on CAT principles, a language encoder) that feed into a lightweight, shared multi-layer projection network. A central coordination module will apply a combined loss function: (1) contrastive losses (e.g., InfoNCE) between aligned modality pairs, (2) reconstruction losses within each modality to preserve fidelity, and (3) a novel coordination loss that penalizes divergence in the shared network's gradients from each modality stream. Training will be end-to-end, balancing these objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training framework that breaks the fidelity-alignment trade-off, yielding encoders that are both high-quality in isolation and perfectly aligned for cross-modal tasks. It will contribute new benchmarks for evaluating unified latent spaces and offer insights into the coordinated optimization of complex, heterogeneous neural modules.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit, gradient-based coordination mechanism during training, which ensures that gains in individual encoder performance are directly leveraged to improve cross-modal alignment, rather than treating these objectives as competing. This moves beyond simple joint training or post-hoc alignment methods prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: MambaFusion: Co-Designing State Space Model Backbones with Content-Adaptive Modality Networks
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal architectures often rely on a transformer backbone with shallow, modality-agnostic projection layers, which can be inefficient and suboptimal for aligning heterogeneous data streams like images, text, and rewards. The rise of efficient sequence models like State Space Models (e.g., Mamba) offers new inductive biases for long-context modeling, but their integration with deep modality-specific processing remains unexplored. A principled co-design is needed to move beyond simple feature concatenation and achieve true architectural synergy for joint generation and understanding tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal model with a core State Space Model (SSM) backbone, co-designed with deep, modality-specific encoders whose internal mechanisms (e.g., selective scanning, recurrence) are informed by the SSM's dynamics, will achieve superior parameter efficiency and performance on joint next-token and diffusion tasks compared to transformer-based models with linear adapters.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture with an SSM (Mamba) as the core sequence mixer. For vision, we will co-design a deep encoder based on the principles of Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization (CAT), but where its patch merging and token pruning mechanisms are guided by the SSM's selective state transition patterns. For language, we will use a deep encoder with structured state updates mirroring the backbone. A key innovation is a 'modality alignment module' that projects these structured features into a latent space optimized for the SSM's linear-time, recurrent processing, rather than for attention. Training will use a joint objective of next-token prediction (language) and diffusion (image) loss, informed by multimodal reward modeling principles for holistic evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, parameter-efficient architecture paradigm for multimodal AI that intrinsically aligns backbone and encoder computational philosophies. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of SSMs over transformers for cohesive multimodal sequence modeling. The resulting model and analysis will offer a blueprint for future modality-coordinated designs beyond the transformer.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in using an SSM backbone, but in the deep, two-way co-design where the SSM's architectural properties (selective scanning, recurrence) dictate the internal mechanisms of deep modality encoders, and their outputs are specifically structured for optimal SSM consumption, moving far beyond simple linear projection layers.

proposals.title: Retrieval-Augmented Transfusion: Enhancing Multi-Modal Generation with External Memory
proposals.motivation: Current large vision-language models (VLMs) are limited by their static, parametric knowledge, leading to factual hallucinations, poor handling of rare concepts, and an inability to incorporate up-to-date information during generation. A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) paradigm, successful in NLP, has not been robustly adapted for dense, multi-modal contexts where retrieved evidence must align across image and text modalities. This gap limits the reliability and knowledge grounding of generated outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting a multi-modal diffusion transformer (Transfusion) with a content-adaptive, cross-modal retrieval mechanism will significantly improve the factual accuracy, detail, and temporal relevance of generated images and text without requiring full model retraining.
proposals.method: We will extend the Transfusion architecture by integrating a retriever module that queries a large-scale, multi-modal memory bank of (image, text) pairs. Building on Yasunaga's CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization), we will develop a joint embedding space for efficient, semantic retrieval of both modalities. Retrieved evidence will be fused into the Transfusion model's cross-attention layers via a novel gating mechanism, conditioned on the query and retrieval relevance scores, to guide the denoising process. Training will involve a two-stage procedure: fine-tuning the retriever and fusion layers using a contrastive loss and a reconstruction reward, informed by insights from Multimodal RewardBench.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, scalable framework for retrieval-augmented multi-modal generation, demonstrated through improved performance on knowledge-intensive image generation and VQA tasks. It will provide new insights into how external memory can mitigate parametric knowledge limitations and distribution shift issues in VLMs, directly addressing concerns highlighted in prior work on OOD detection.
proposals.novelty: While RAG exists for language, this proposal uniquely integrates it into a diffusion-based multi-modal *generation* model (Transfusion) using content-adaptive tokenization for retrieval, creating a closed-loop system where retrieval influences both image and text synthesis dynamically.

proposals.title: EfficientScale-MM: Data-Efficient Architectures and Scaling Laws for Emergent Multimodal Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art multimodal models achieve broad capabilities through massive scaling of parameters and data, which is environmentally and economically unsustainable. This brute-force paradigm often overlooks the inherent compositional structure and complementary signals present across modalities like text, image, and audio. A fundamental re-evaluation of how architectural efficiency interacts with multimodal data structure is needed to reduce the compute barrier for advanced AI.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing novel, data-efficient architectures that explicitly model cross-modal interactions with efficient training objectives, we can trigger the emergence of sophisticated multimodal understanding and generation capabilities at a significantly reduced parameter and compute scale compared to standard dense scaling.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture integrating modality-specific token compressors (inspired by CAT for images) with a sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) backbone where experts are conditioned on cross-modal context. Training will employ a multi-stage curriculum: first, modality-specific autoencoding; second, cross-modal contrastive alignment; and third, a unified objective combining generative and discriminative rewards (informed by Multimodal RewardBench). We will systematically vary model scale, data diversity, and architectural hyperparameters to derive new scaling laws for capability emergence.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel, publicly released multimodal architecture demonstrating strong performance at sub-10B parameters, 2) a new set of empirical scaling laws that quantify the relationship between architectural efficiency, data composition, and emergent capabilities, and 3) a framework for evaluating whether models are learning genuine cross-modal composition or merely correlational patterns.
proposals.novelty: While efficient architectures (e.g., MoE) and modality-specific tokenization exist, their co-design for explicit cross-modal efficiency and the systematic study of resulting scaling laws for capability emergence is unexplored. This moves beyond simple cost reduction to a principled investigation of how data structure can reduce necessary scale.

proposals.title: Adversarial Task Priors: Hybrid Pretraining for Targeted Capability with Certified Robustness in Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language model (VLM) pretraining faces a critical trade-off: broad, general pretraining is robust but inefficient for specific tasks, while narrow, task-informed pretraining yields high in-distribution performance but suffers from catastrophic failures under distributional shift and adversarial attacks. This brittleness limits the safe deployment of high-performance VLMs in real-world applications. The problem is exacerbated by the lack of training paradigms that systematically build robustness into models optimized for a target capability.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid pretraining paradigm that jointly optimizes for high task-specific accuracy and certified robustness via a two-stream adversarial process can produce VLMs that are both highly capable on a target task and systematically resistant to distributional shifts and adversarial manipulation.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream pretraining framework for VLMs. The primary stream uses curated data for the target task (e.g., medical VQA). The adversarial stream employs a generative model (e.g., a diffusion model) or a retrieval system to synthesize or retrieve challenging examples that maximally disrupt the model's emerging task priors. The core training objective is a meta-learning loss that balances standard cross-entropy on the primary stream with a certified robustness loss (inspired by randomized smoothing or interval bound propagation techniques) computed on the adversarial stream's outputs. This creates a dynamic robustness benchmark that evolves with the model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A novel hybrid pretraining framework that integrates adversarial data generation with certified robustness optimization for VLMs. 2) New benchmarks and metrics for evaluating the trade-off between targeted capability and systematic robustness. 3) Open-source models and code demonstrating superior robustness-accuracy Pareto frontiers compared to standard fine-tuning or adversarial training baselines.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of a dynamically generated adversarial data stream with certified robustness guarantees within a meta-learning pretraining objective, moving beyond post-hoc adversarial training or static robustness evaluations to bake robustness directly into the task-specific pretraining process.

proposals.title: Vaccine-Inspired Pretraining: Curated Exposure to Failure Modes for Robust Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale pretraining for vision-language models (VLMs) relies on broad, undifferentiated data, which can lead to brittle performance on critical tasks and known failure modes, such as adversarial attacks or systematic reasoning errors. This paradigm mirrors a naive immune system exposed to all pathogens without strategic priming, potentially missing targeted robustness. A more controlled, immunology-inspired approach to model development is needed to build intrinsic safety and reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: A targeted pretraining phase using curated datasets representing specific failure modes or critical domains will yield VLMs with superior robustness and performance on those targeted challenges compared to models trained solely on broad, task-agnostic data.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage pipeline: first, a standard broad pretraining phase, followed by a targeted 'vaccination' phase. We will curate datasets for known VLM vulnerabilities (e.g., visual illusions, adversarial image-text pairs, complex compositional reasoning) and critical safety domains. Building on methods like CAT for adaptive tokenization and insights from Multimodal RewardBench for evaluation, we will fine-tune the model on these curated sets using a contrastive or reward-augmented objective to strengthen specific 'immunological' pathways. Performance will be rigorously evaluated against baselines using targeted adversarial benchmarks and standard multimodal tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, biologically-inspired paradigm for building robust AI systems, moving beyond post-hoc fixes. It will provide a methodological framework for targeted pretraining and deliver empirical evidence on whether controlled exposure to failure modes can confer intrinsic robustness, potentially reducing the need for extensive safety fine-tuning later.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the core assumption of purely task-agnostic pretraining by proposing a strategic, problem-informed priming stage, an idea largely unexplored in multimodal learning. It formally adapts a vaccine analogy into a concrete machine learning methodology for robustness.

proposals.title: Plug-and-Play Modality Interfaces: Decoupling Core Reasoning from Input Processing for Stable Post-Deployment Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current adaptation of large multimodal models (LMMs) to new data types (e.g., high-res imagery, 3D point clouds) typically requires full or partial fine-tuning, risking catastrophic forgetting of core knowledge and incurring high computational cost per modality. This limits the practical, iterative enhancement of deployed models. A method is needed to integrate new sensory inputs without destabilizing the model's foundational reasoning capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a foundational model's core knowledge can be preserved by decoupling it from input processing via a standardized, learnable interface, allowing high-fidelity adaptation to new modalities through external, pluggable encoder-decoder units that project data into the model's native latent space.
proposals.method: We will design a standardized, low-dimensional bottleneck interface (inspired by adapter layers) between the core model and external modules. For each new modality (e.g., spectral data), we will train a compact, autoencoder-like unit with a reconstruction loss to map raw data to/from this interface. The core model's weights remain frozen. We will leverage techniques from CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) for efficient encoding and use RewardBench-inspired protocols to holistically evaluate the impact on downstream task performance and out-of-distribution robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical framework for post-deployment model extension, reducing adaptation costs and preserving model stability. It will provide empirical evidence on the composability of modality-specific units and establish evaluation benchmarks for modular multimodal adaptation, moving beyond single-modality fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adapter-based methods that modify internal model pathways, this approach proposes fully external, swappable processing units with a standardized interface, treating modality adaptation as a compression/reconstruction problem decoupled from the core model's reasoning network.

proposals.title: Rational Data Curation for Vision-Language Models: A Pharmaceutical-Inspired Paradigm for Safety and Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale vision-language model (VLM) training relies on massive, often noisy web-scale datasets, which can inadvertently embed harmful biases, toxic content, and factual inaccuracies. This scale-centric paradigm creates a fundamental tension between model capability and safety, as scaling data quantity often amplifies these risks. A new data strategy is needed to decouple capability from harmful content, analogous to the shift in drug discovery from high-throughput screening to rational, quality-focused library design.
proposals.hypothesis: A meticulously curated, quality-weighted dataset, where each data point is validated for factual accuracy, ethical alignment, and pedagogical utility, can act as a data efficiency multiplier, enabling the training of VLMs with superior safety and alignment properties using significantly less total data than current scale-driven approaches.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel dataset, 'CuratedVision', by applying pharmaceutical-inspired curation principles: purity (filtering toxic/biased content via classifiers), known relevance (ensuring factual grounding via knowledge bases), and structural diversity (covering compositional concepts). We will train a series of VLMs (e.g., CLIP-ViT/L-14 architecture) on progressively larger subsets of CuratedVision and standard web-scale data (e.g., LAION), controlling for total tokens. Model safety and capability will be evaluated using a holistic benchmark suite combining established safety probes (e.g., red-teaming templates), our Multimodal RewardBench for alignment, and standard performance tasks (e.g., VQAv2, OK-VQA).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence for the efficacy of rational data curation over brute-force scaling in multimodal AI. It will deliver a publicly released, high-quality dataset with provenance metadata and a framework for quantifying the 'data quality multiplier' effect on safety-performance trade-offs.
proposals.novelty: While dataset filtering exists, this proposal's novelty lies in its direct analogy to rational drug design, framing data curation as a systematic engineering of pedagogical utility and safety, and its rigorous, controlled test of the quality-vs-quantity hypothesis for VLMs—a question largely unexplored in multimodal contexts compared to language-only models.

proposals.title: Edge-Universal: A Single Foundation Model for Multi-Modal Edge AI via Unified Information-Theoretic Compression
proposals.motivation: Current multi-modal AI relies on stitching large, separate models for vision, language, and audio, creating massive systems unsuitable for edge deployment. This forces reliance on cloud APIs, raising latency, cost, and privacy concerns. A truly efficient, unified model trained from scratch for on-device use remains an open challenge, critical for enabling next-generation ubiquitous AI applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, radically efficient transformer-based model, trained from scratch with a novel loss function that generalizes information-theoretic compression across discrete tokens and continuous signals, can learn a shared, compressed representation enabling competitive multi-modal reasoning and generation directly on edge hardware.
proposals.method: We will design a unified loss function based on rate-distortion and perceptual similarity, extending principles from vector quantization and diffusion to handle text tokens, image patches (leveraging insights from CAT for adaptive tokenization), and audio frames within a single objective. The model architecture will be a sparse mixture-of-experts transformer with modality-agnostic early layers and task-specific expert routing, heavily optimized for parameter efficiency. Training will be performed from scratch on a curated multi-modal dataset (image-text-audio triplets) using this unified loss, with reward modeling techniques (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench) to further align model outputs for downstream edge tasks.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, theoretically-grounded unified loss for multi-modal learning. 2) An open-source, sub-500M parameter foundation model demonstrating viable on-device multi-modal capabilities. 3) A new benchmark for evaluating the efficiency and performance of edge-native multi-modal models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that combines pre-trained modality-specific encoders, we co-design the loss and architecture for joint training from scratch, targeting a unified compressed representation. This moves beyond simple fusion to intrinsic multi-modal efficiency, a departure from the dominant scaling paradigm.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Inversion for Forensic Analysis of Multi-Modal Training Data
proposals.motivation: As large-scale multi-modal models (e.g., Transfusion, Flamingo) trained on proprietary datasets become ubiquitous, auditing their training data for biases, imbalances, and provenance is a critical challenge. Current forensic methods, like membership inference or dataset reconstruction, are largely unimodal and cannot exploit the rich cross-modal correlations learned by these models. This lack of tools hinders transparency and responsible development in multi-modal AI.
proposals.hypothesis: The learned joint embedding space of a unified multi-modal model encodes statistical footprints of its training data distribution, enabling the inversion of properties (e.g., class distributions, spurious correlations) in one modality (e.g., video) via probing with another, more structured modality (e.g., text).
proposals.method: We will extend the concept of training data distillation and gradient-based inversion to multi-modal settings. Building on architectures like Transfusion and leveraging adaptive tokenization (e.g., CAT), we will develop a cross-modal inversion loss. This loss will optimize a synthetic dataset in a target modality (e.g., audio spectrograms) to maximize the alignment of its embeddings with a controlled set of query embeddings from a source modality (e.g., text descriptions of demographic or acoustic concepts). We will validate the method using controlled, known datasets like AudioSet and HowTo100M, measuring how well inverted statistics match the true training data properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated framework for cross-modal training data forensics, providing a practical tool for dataset auditing without direct access. It will contribute new benchmarks for evaluating multi-modal model transparency and offer insights into how cross-modal correlations are encoded in model parameters.
proposals.novelty: While data forensics and inversion exist for unimodal models, applying them *across* modalities to infer training data properties is unexplored. This directly leverages the unified representation learning central to modern multi-modal architectures, moving beyond treating each modality in isolation.

proposals.title: MIB-Scale: Scaling a Unified Modality-Invariant Bottleneck for Coherent Multimodal Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models often scale by adding separate, modality-specific encoders and fusion layers, leading to fragmented representations and inefficient cross-modal generalization. This architecture-centric scaling complicates the addition of new modalities and fails to build a unified, abstract understanding of the underlying concepts shared across sensory inputs. A more principled approach is needed to achieve scalable, coherent multimodal intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling the capacity and expressiveness of a single, modality-invariant latent bottleneck, enforced through novel training objectives, will yield a model whose core conceptual understanding scales more effectively with the number of integrated modalities than scaling the count of modality-specific components.
proposals.method: We will develop an architecture with a shared transformer-based bottleneck layer. Inspired by contrastive and adversarial learning, we will introduce a 'modality-discrimination' loss that penalizes the bottleneck for retaining modality-specific signals, alongside a robust 'content-alignment' loss (e.g., a multimodal variant of reward modeling from RewardBench) that rewards the representation for capturing semantic content. We will leverage adaptive tokenization methods (extending CAT principles) to create a more uniform input space for non-text modalities before the bottleneck.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for scalable multimodal integration, moving beyond fusion of experts. We will deliver a new benchmark for evaluating modality-invariance and conceptual coherence across diverse inputs, alongside an open-source model demonstrating stronger zero-shot generalization to unseen modality combinations compared to contemporary fusion-based architectures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored shared representations, our approach uniquely focuses on scaling the bottleneck itself as the primary lever for improvement, using adversarial training specifically to strip modality, and employs multimodal reward modeling to directly shape the abstract conceptual content, a combination not yet explored.

proposals.title: Scaffold-VLM: Scaling Multimodal Integration via Dynamic Cross-Modal Routing
proposals.motivation: Current large vision-language models (VLMs) scale primarily by increasing parameter counts within isolated encoders and fusion modules, leading to sub-linear gains in complex multimodal reasoning. This mirrors a biological scaling problem where simply adding cells does not create a functional organ; a guiding scaffold is needed. We posit that the next frontier in multimodal AI is not raw capacity but the depth and structure of cross-modal integration, which requires a novel architectural paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, sparse computational scaffold that explicitly orchestrates cross-modal interactions will enable more sample-efficient training and superior scaling laws for multimodal reasoning compared to scaling dense, modality-specific parameters alone.
proposals.method: We will design a Cross-Modal Scaffolding (CMS) layer, a sparse, gated routing network that sits between pretrained unimodal encoders (e.g., a ViT, LLM). Inspired by mixture-of-experts and dynamic neural networks, the CMS will learn to activate sparse, content-adaptive pathways between modality-specific features, guided by a reward model trained on holistic tasks from datasets like Multimodal RewardBench.
