Training will involve a two-stage process: first, warm-starting encoders, then jointly training the scaffold and a lightweight fusion head with a regularization loss to encourage sparsity and prevent collapse.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new architectural blueprint for scaling multimodal integration, moving beyond simple fusion. We will release a scalable model, Scaffold-VLM, and a benchmark for evaluating integration depth, offering new insights into the sample efficiency and scaling laws of structured multimodal systems.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on better fusion or tokenization (e.g., CAT), we explicitly model integration as a sparse, dynamic routing problem, drawing a direct analogy to biological scaffolding to guide architectural scaling—a perspective not explored in current VLM literature.

proposals.title: StressFusion: Co-Designing Scaled Multimodal Architectures and Adversarial Benchmarks to Expose Cross-Modal Fragility
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models (VLMs) are evaluated on static benchmarks that often miss subtle cross-modal inconsistencies and reasoning failures, especially as modalities scale beyond vision-language. As we integrate audio, video, and other sensory signals, existing fusion methods and evaluations are insufficient to diagnose emergent, compound failure modes, creating a false sense of robustness. This gap is critical for deploying reliable systems in complex, real-world environments.
proposals.hypothesis: Deliberately scaling multimodal integration with diverse fusion mechanisms, when paired with targeted adversarial stress testing, will systematically expose and characterize fundamental architectural brittleness that standard benchmarks fail to reveal.
proposals.method: We will construct a scalable multimodal transformer architecture capable of ingesting up to 5 modalities (image, text, audio, video, symbolic). We will implement and ablate fusion mechanisms (e.g., late, early, cross-attention). Concurrently, we will develop Multimodal StressBench, a suite of adversarial evaluations generating cross-modal conflicts (e.g., misleading audio for a video, contradictory text for a graph) and distribution shifts. We will train models on standard data and then probe them with StressBench, using the failure patterns to iteratively redesign the fusion architecture.
proposals.contribution: 1) A diagnostic framework (Multimodal StressBench) for rigorously evaluating cross-modal consistency under adversarial conditions. 2) A set of principled findings on the failure modes of different fusion strategies as modality count increases, guiding future robust architecture design. 3) A new, publicly available scaled model that serves as a testbed for fragility research.
proposals.novelty: Unlike scaling for general performance, we scale integration specifically to induce and study failure, creating a tight feedback loop where adversarial evaluation directly informs architectural scaling decisions, a co-design approach not yet explored.

proposals.title: Multimodal Generalist Evaluation: A Unified Benchmark for Vision-Language and Audio-Language Reward Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of large multimodal models (LMMs) is fragmented, focusing on narrow task-specific performance. There is a critical lack of holistic, generalist benchmarks for evaluating the core alignment component—reward models—across diverse multimodal inputs. This gap hinders the systematic development of safe and capable LMMs, as we cannot reliably measure their preference modeling and instruction-following robustness in complex, real-world multimodal scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified, multi-domain evaluation framework for multimodal reward models will reveal significant performance gaps and distributional inconsistencies not apparent in single-modality or task-specific benchmarks, and that content-adaptive tokenization is a key factor in cross-modal generalization.
proposals.method: We will extend the RewardBench methodology to multimodal settings, creating a curated suite of vision-language and audio-language preference pairs. The benchmark will include in-distribution (ID) and out-of-distribution (OOD) splits across domains like detailed captioning, safety, and complex reasoning. We will evaluate state-of-the-art reward models, analyzing their correlation with downstream LMM performance. Crucially, we will integrate and ablate different tokenization strategies (e.g., CAT for images, audio spectrogram patches) to isolate their impact on reward model accuracy and generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, publicly available benchmark for evaluating multimodal reward models, enabling direct comparison and diagnosing failure modes. It will deliver actionable insights into how tokenization and training data composition affect cross-modal alignment, guiding the development of more robust and generalist LMMs.
proposals.novelty: While RewardBench exists for language, and multimodal benchmarks focus on end-task performance, this proposal uniquely targets the evaluation of the *preference models* themselves across modalities, explicitly linking tokenization efficiency to reward modeling robustness.

proposals.title: Spider-X: Scaling and Diversifying Text-to-SQL Evaluation with Cross-Modal Domain Augmentation
proposals.motivation: The Spider dataset is the standard benchmark for complex, cross-domain text-to-SQL, but its scale and domain diversity are increasingly insufficient for evaluating modern large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) that exhibit strong in-context learning. This limitation obscures true model capabilities and failure modes in real-world, data-rich scenarios. Expanding Spider is crucial for robust evaluation, especially for multimodal systems that must reason over structured data described in text or images.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically augmenting Spider with new databases and queries, generated via a content-adaptive, reward-guided synthesis pipeline, will expose significant performance gaps in current text-to-SQL models and enable more reliable evaluation of their generalization.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage augmentation pipeline. First, we will use LLMs (e.g., GPT-4) guided by a multimodal reward model—inspired by Multimodal RewardBench—to generate novel, semantically valid database schemas and associated natural language questions across diverse, under-represented domains (e.g., scientific, IoT). Second, we will synthesize corresponding SQL queries using constrained decoding and verify their executability. The process will leverage CAT-style tokenization principles for efficient schema representation and apply out-of-distribution detection methods to ensure the novel queries present a distinct challenge from the original Spider distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver Spider-X, a significantly larger and more diverse public benchmark for text-to-SQL, enabling finer-grained analysis of model performance. It will provide new insights into the relationship between training data diversity, reward model guidance, and compositional generalization in structured prediction tasks for language and multimodal models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior text-to-SQL dataset efforts, this proposal uniquely integrates reward model guidance from multimodal RLHF and content-adaptive tokenization strategies for schema representation to control the quality and diversity of the generated data, moving beyond simple template-based or rule-based expansion.

proposals.title: META-SQL: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Cross-Domain Text-to-SQL Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) struggle to generate accurate SQL queries for unseen database schemas, especially with limited examples. This hinders the deployment of natural language interfaces to databases in real-world settings where schemas are diverse and labeled data is scarce. A model that can rapidly adapt to new schemas with minimal supervision is a critical unsolved problem in multimodal knowledge grounding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, trained on a distribution of database schema adaptation tasks, will enable a vision-language model to learn a prior for fast adaptation, significantly outperforming standard fine-tuning on few-shot cross-domain Text-to-SQL tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework where each 'task' is defined by a unique database schema. The base model will be a pre-trained multimodal encoder (e.g., based on CAT for schema structure tokenization) combined with a language decoder. The inner-loop optimization will adapt the model's parameters using a few (k-shot) <natural language, SQL> examples from a new schema, while the outer-loop meta-update will train the model's initial parameters to be highly responsive to such adaptation. We will benchmark on the Spider dataset, simulating real-world unseen schemas.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel meta-learning benchmark and protocol for cross-domain Text-to-SQL, 2) a model capable of few-shot adaptation to novel database structures, advancing robust human-database interaction, and 3) insights into how meta-learning can improve the generalization of multimodal models for structured knowledge tasks.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored in NLP, its application to cross-domain Text-to-SQL is novel, particularly when integrating recent advances in content-adaptive tokenization (CAT) for schema representation and evaluating adaptation performance with holistic reward metrics (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench).

proposals.title: MED-VALIGN: Weakly-Supervised Visual-Language Alignment for Medical Imaging via Cross-Modal Cycle Consistency and Reward-Based Refinement
proposals.motivation: High-quality, paired image-text datasets are scarce in specialized domains like medicine, limiting the development of multimodal models. Current vision-language models (VLMs) rely heavily on such curated data, which is expensive and time-consuming to produce. Learning from abundant but unpaired or weakly-labeled medical images and reports presents a critical, unsolved challenge for scalable medical AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A model can learn robust visual-language alignment for medical imaging by enforcing semantic consistency across cycles of image-to-text-to-image and text-to-image-to-text generation, and that this alignment can be refined using a domain-specific reward model trained on limited high-quality pairs.
proposals.method: We will extend the Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization (CAT) framework to efficiently encode medical images. A primary model will be trained with a cross-modal cycle consistency loss, reconstructing original image features/text from generated text/image captions. Concurrently, we will train a compact medical reward model on the limited paired data (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench principles) to score alignment quality. This reward will be used to fine-tune the primary model via reinforcement learning, prioritizing medically plausible associations over spurious correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training paradigm for medical VLMs that reduces dependency on perfect image-text pairs. It will provide a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for weakly-supervised medical multimodal learning, assessing both generation quality and clinical reasoning. The method aims to set a new performance standard on tasks like medical visual question answering and report generation when using weakly-labeled data.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines cross-modal cycle consistency—a form of self-supervision for unpaired data—with targeted reward-driven refinement using a small curated set, moving beyond simple contrastive pre-training. It directly addresses the out-of-distribution challenge in medical data by building robustness into the alignment process from the start.

proposals.title: MED-SLIM: Few-Shot Medical Vision-Language Learning via Efficient Architecture Search and Adaptive Reward Tuning
proposals.motivation: Large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) are data-hungry and computationally prohibitive for specialized domains like medicine, where annotated data is scarce and deployment requires efficiency. The prevailing paradigm of scaling up base models for few-shot performance creates unsustainable barriers to clinical adaptation and research iteration. This work challenges the necessity of large base models by asking if superior few-shot medical VLM performance can be achieved through specialized, efficient architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a small, domain-optimized VLM architecture, combined with content-adaptive tokenization and targeted reward-based tuning on limited medical data, can match or exceed the few-shot diagnostic and reasoning performance of much larger general-purpose base models.
proposals.method: We will design a search space of efficient, modular encoder-decoder architectures (e.g., combining compact vision backbones like MobileNetV3 with small language models). We will integrate Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization (CAT) to compress medical images without losing diagnostic detail. The core training will use a two-stage process: 1) contrastive pre-training on a small, curated medical image-text corpus, and 2) few-shot fine-tuning guided by a multimodal reward model (inspired by RewardBench principles) to optimize for clinical accuracy and reasoning over mere generative fluency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete blueprint for building high-performance, data-efficient medical VLMs, reducing computational barriers for healthcare AI. It will deliver a publicly released model and benchmark demonstrating that architectural specialization and targeted tuning can invert the need for scale in data-scarce domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically decoupling model scale from few-shot medical VLM performance by co-designing efficient architecture search with adaptive tokenization and reward-driven tuning—a departure from the dominant approach of starting with a frozen, massive base model.

proposals.title: Beyond Generation: A Discriminative and Retrieval-Based Framework for Robust Medical Visual Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current medical VQA evaluation heavily relies on generative models, which can produce fluent but factually incorrect or hallucinated answers, posing serious risks in clinical settings. Pre-2026, there is a lack of systematic comparison between generative, discriminative (e.g., multiple-choice), and retrieval-based paradigms for medical VQA, despite known challenges in model calibration and out-of-distribution generalization. This gap prevents the identification of the most reliable and safe evaluation methodology for domain-specific tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: For closed-domain, fact-based medical VQA tasks, discriminative and retrieval-based evaluation paradigms will demonstrate superior accuracy, robustness to out-of-distribution queries, and better alignment with expert judgment compared to open-ended generative evaluation.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled benchmark from existing datasets (e.g., VQA-RAD, PathVQA) with tasks categorized by reasoning type (fact recall, abnormality detection, differential diagnosis). We will adapt and rigorously compare three families of models: 1) Generative VLMs (e.g., BLIP-2, LLaVA), 2) Discriminative models using a contrastive vision-language encoder (like CLIP) fine-tuned for multiple-choice classification, and 3) A retrieval-based system that indexes a knowledge base of (image, caption, QA) tuples using content-adaptive image tokenization (CAT) and dense text retrieval (e.g., DPR). Performance will be measured by standard accuracy and robustness metrics from pre-2026 OOD detection literature, analyzing failure modes across paradigms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical framework for selecting VQA evaluation paradigms based on medical task characteristics, directly informing safer AI deployment. It will yield a public benchmark suite and analysis of trade-offs between fluency, factual precision, and robustness, advancing the field of multimodal medical AI evaluation beyond simple generative performance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work critiques generative evaluation, this proposal systematically pits it against discriminative and retrieval-based approaches within medical VQA, leveraging Yasunaga's expertise in multimodal reward modeling and adaptive tokenization to build a more holistic assessment framework.

proposals.title: MedBench-FS: A Challenging Benchmark for Few-Shot Medical Visual Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Current medical VQA benchmarks often evaluate models in a fully supervised setting, failing to assess their true few-shot generalization capabilities, which is critical for deployment in specialized domains with limited labeled data. Existing benchmarks lack systematic variation in question complexity, imaging modality, and clinical reasoning depth, making it difficult to diagnose model weaknesses. A more comprehensive and challenging few-shot benchmark is needed to drive progress towards robust, data-efficient medical AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current state-of-the-art multimodal models, when evaluated on a benchmark designed with explicit axes of difficulty (e.g., compositional reasoning, rare findings, cross-modality inference), will reveal significant and previously unmeasured performance gaps in few-shot settings.
proposals.method: We will construct MedBench-FS by curating and augmenting existing public datasets (e.g., VQA-RAD, PathVQA, MIMIC-CXR) to create a stratified benchmark. It will feature controlled few-shot splits across key axes: reasoning type (descriptive, diagnostic, interpretive), modality (X-ray, pathology, dermatology), and data distribution shifts. Evaluation will leverage the researcher's expertise in reward modeling and OOD detection by incorporating metrics for answer uncertainty, calibration under distribution shift, and consistency of reasoning, inspired by Multimodal RewardBench and OOD detection critiques. We will benchmark modern VLMs (e.g., LLaVA, Flamingo variants) fine-tuned with few-shot examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, challenging benchmark for few-shot medical VQA, enabling more rigorous model comparison. It will produce a detailed failure mode analysis of current VLMs, identifying specific gaps in clinical reasoning and data efficiency. The benchmark's design principles will inform the creation of future evaluation suites for specialized domains.
proposals.novelty: Unlike existing medical VQA benchmarks, MedBench-FS is explicitly designed for the few-shot regime with structured axes of difficulty, integrating evaluation of reasoning consistency and uncertainty—dimensions highlighted as critical by the researcher's prior work on reward models and OOD detection. It moves beyond aggregate accuracy to a diagnostic evaluation framework.

proposals.title: Test-Time Training for Vision-Language Models: Few-Shot Adaptation via Self-Supervised Reward
proposals.motivation: Large vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with distribution shifts at inference, performing poorly on novel tasks or data domains not seen during training. Pre-2026 adaptation methods like fine-tuning require extensive labeled data and computational resources, making them impractical for dynamic, real-world deployment. Test-time training (TTT), which adapts a model using only its test input, offers a promising but underexplored path for efficient VLM adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VLM can be effectively adapted at test-time using a self-supervised reward signal derived from its own multimodal outputs, enabling robust few-shot performance on out-of-distribution tasks without task-specific pre-training.
proposals.method: We will build upon the CAT tokenizer and a base VLM (e.g., CLIP or BLIP architecture) to create a TTT framework. At inference, given a few in-context examples, the model will generate candidate outputs for a new query. A lightweight, frozen reward model (inspired by Multimodal RewardBench principles) will score these candidates based on multimodal alignment. This reward signal will guide a limited, efficient parameter update (e.g., via soft prompt tuning or adapter layers) specifically for the current test batch, using a contrastive or policy gradient objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel TTT algorithm for VLMs that enables practical few-shot adaptation. It will provide new insights into the relationship between reward modeling, OOD robustness, and test-time learning. We will release a benchmark for evaluating TTT in VLMs, extending analysis beyond traditional OOD detection metrics.
proposals.novelty: While TTT has been studied for unimodal vision tasks, its application to multimodal VLMs using self-supervised reward for adaptation is novel. This approach uniquely combines test-time learning with principles from reward modeling and efficient tokenization, moving beyond simple entropy minimization.

proposals.title: MedCLIP-T: Transferring Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training for Robust Medical Vision-Language Alignment
proposals.motivation: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) are crucial for tasks like report generation and visual question answering, but they often underperform due to the domain gap between natural and medical images. Pre-2026, contrastive pre-training (e.g., CLIP) has shown promise for alignment, but its direct application to medicine is hampered by limited, specialized paired data and poor generalization to rare conditions. This work addresses the need for data-efficient and robust alignment methods tailored to the medical domain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-stage transfer contrastive learning framework, which first adapts a general-domain VLM using a content-adaptive tokenizer and then refines it with a novel distribution-aware contrastive loss, will yield medical image-text representations with superior alignment and out-of-distribution robustness compared to standard fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will build upon the CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) framework to efficiently encode medical images. The method involves: 1) Initial adaptation of a pre-trained VLM (e.g., a CLIP variant) using a standard contrastive loss on a broad medical dataset (e.g., MIMIC-CXR). 2) A second-stage training with a novel 'hard negative mining' contrastive objective, informed by principles from Out-of-Distribution detection research, to specifically improve alignment for semantically similar but pathologically distinct cases (e.g., different types of lesions). Performance will be evaluated on Medical RewardBench-style diagnostic VQA and retrieval tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, parameter-efficient framework for transferring contrastive VLMs to the medical domain. It will provide empirical evidence on the utility of specialized tokenization and distribution-aware contrastive losses for medical alignment, and release a benchmark for evaluating the robustness of medical VLMs to challenging, fine-grained visual concepts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the structured transfer of contrastive pre-training specifically for medicine, combining content-adaptive tokenization (CAT) with a novel contrastive loss designed to address medical OOD challenges, moving beyond simple fine-tuning of monolithic models like CLIP.

proposals.title: MedScale: Scaling Vision-Language Pre-training with Curated Multimodal Medical Data for Holistic Clinical Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current vision-language models (VLMs) for medicine are often fine-tuned from general-domain models, lacking the depth of biomedical knowledge and reasoning required for complex clinical tasks. While datasets like MIMIC-CXR exist, scaling model size and pre-training on a massive, curated corpus of multimodal medical data (e.g., radiology images with reports, pathology slides, clinical notes) remains underexplored. This gap limits the development of models with robust, generalizable medical understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling up a VLM's parameter count and pre-training it on a large-scale, carefully curated multimodal medical corpus will lead to significant improvements in holistic clinical reasoning, outperforming fine-tuned models on a broad spectrum of diagnostic and interpretive tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive, de-identified corpus integrating diverse medical modalities (radiology, pathology, dermatology) with aligned text. We will scale a transformer-based VLM architecture (e.g., a CLIP-style encoder or a unified encoder-decoder) to tens of billions of parameters. Pre-training will leverage a multi-objective loss combining contrastive image-text alignment, masked language modeling, and a novel tokenization strategy inspired by CAT (Content-Adaptive Image Tokenization) to efficiently process high-resolution medical images. Training stability at scale will be managed using techniques like sequence parallelism and mixed precision.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver MedScale, the largest openly documented VLM pre-trained specifically on multimodal medical data. We will contribute a rigorous evaluation using a bespoke benchmark extending principles from Multimodal RewardBench to assess clinical reasoning, factual accuracy, and robustness to out-of-distribution medical cases. The model and benchmark will serve as a foundation for advanced medical AI research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic scaling of model size and a curated, multimodal medical pre-training corpus at an unprecedented scale, moving beyond fine-tuning. It uniquely integrates scalable architecture design with domain-adaptive tokenization (CAT) and holistic evaluation informed by reward modeling principles, targeting fundamental clinical reasoning rather than narrow task performance.

proposals.title: CLINIC-GEN: Content-Adaptive, Reward-Guided Generation of Medical Images from Clinical Text
proposals.motivation: Generating accurate medical images from clinical text can revolutionize medical education, clinical planning, and data augmentation for rare conditions. However, pre-2026 text-to-image models (e.g., diffusion models) lack domain-specific precision for medicine and produce outputs that are often anatomically implausible or misaligned with detailed clinical descriptors. This gap limits their utility in high-stakes healthcare applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A text-to-image generation model that integrates a content-adaptive visual tokenizer for medical structures with a multimodal reward model for clinical alignment will produce more accurate and clinically relevant images than standard domain-agnostic models.
proposals.method: We will build upon a diffusion-based architecture (e.g., Latent Diffusion Model). First, we will adapt the Content-Adaptive Tokenization (CAT) method to create a specialized visual tokenizer for medical imaging modalities (X-ray, MRI). Second, we will train the model on paired clinical notes and images from datasets like MIMIC-CXR. Third, we will integrate a multimodal reward model, inspired by Multimodal RewardBench, fine-tuned on clinician feedback to score and guide the generation for anatomical correctness and textual alignment, using techniques like reward-weighted likelihood or reinforcement learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, clinically-aligned image generation model and a benchmark for evaluating medical text-to-image fidelity. It will advance methods for integrating domain-specific tokenization and reward learning into generative pipelines, providing a blueprint for other specialized domains.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic integration of content-adaptive tokenization (for medical structure efficiency) with a multimodal reward model (for clinical alignment), moving beyond simple text conditioning to enforce detailed, verifiable clinical constraints during generation.

proposals.title: HELM-Live: A Framework for Continuous, Automated Benchmarking of Evolving Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: The Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) benchmark provides a crucial, standardized assessment of model capabilities. However, the rapid emergence of new multimodal models, tasks, and failure modes renders static benchmarks obsolete quickly, creating a gap in our ability to track and understand model evolution systematically. A living benchmark is needed to provide timely, comparable evaluations that reflect the state of the art.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous, automated update framework for HELM, integrating novel multimodal scenarios and reward-based evaluation metrics, will yield more accurate, timely, and actionable insights into model capabilities and limitations than static snapshot evaluations.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated pipeline to ingest and evaluate new open-source and API-based VLMs on HELM's core scenarios. We will extend the benchmark by integrating novel, challenging multimodal scenarios (e.g., compositional reasoning, temporal understanding) and new evaluation metrics, particularly drawing from reward model and OOD detection research (e.g., using reward models as refined judges for open-ended generation, applying OOD principles to detect scenario drift). The framework will use a versioned, public leaderboard and dataset to ensure reproducibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a continuously updated, trusted source of model performance data, enabling more dynamic research and model development. It will also contribute a methodological framework for maintaining living benchmarks and demonstrate new evaluation paradigms combining reward models with holistic benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While HELM is established, the proposal to operationalize it as a continuously updated, automated system with integrated reward-based and OOD-inspired metrics is novel. This moves beyond periodic manual updates to a principled, living evaluation ecosystem.

proposals.title: MultiRewardBench: A Holistic Framework for Evaluating Multilingual and Multimodal Reward Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation frameworks for reward models (RMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), such as the recent Multimodal RewardBench, are predominantly English-centric. This creates a significant gap in understanding model safety, alignment, and performance for the vast majority of the world's languages and diverse multimodal interactions (e.g., text-audio, video-text). As AI systems are deployed globally, a holistic, multilingual and multimodal evaluation suite is critical to identify and mitigate performance disparities and safety risks across languages and modalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current reward models and VLMs exhibit significant and systematic performance degradation, as well as inconsistent safety alignment, when evaluated across non-English languages and under-sampled multimodal combinations, which cannot be captured by English-only or vision-language-only benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the Multimodal RewardBench framework by: 1) Curating a high-quality dataset spanning multiple languages (e.g., Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic, Hindi) and modalities (audio, video, 3D), adapting existing safety and preference categories. 2) Developing and applying content-adaptive tokenization methods (inspired by CAT) to efficiently handle diverse input signals. 3) Systematically evaluating a suite of state-of-the-art multilingual/multimodal models (e.g., from the Qwen-VL, SeamlessM4T, and ImageBind lineages) using this benchmark, analyzing performance gaps and failure modes across axes of language, modality, and task.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark for holistically evaluating reward models and VLMs across languages and modalities. It will yield actionable insights into the cross-lingual and cross-modal generalization capabilities of modern models, informing the development of more robust and equitable alignment techniques. The resulting public benchmark will serve as a critical tool for the research community.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created English-centric multimodal benchmarks or monolingual text evaluations, this proposal uniquely integrates multilingual and truly multimodal (beyond vision-language) evaluation into a single, holistic framework for reward model assessment, directly addressing a critical gap in alignment research.

proposals.title: CrowdEval: Human-in-the-Loop Discovery of Dynamic Evaluation Scenarios and Metrics for Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) relies on static benchmarks, which quickly become saturated and fail to capture novel, real-world failure modes. This creates a misleading performance gap between benchmark results and actual deployment, as highlighted by research on out-of-distribution detection. A dynamic, human-driven evaluation framework is needed to continuously stress-test models and uncover unforeseen weaknesses.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured crowdsourcing platform can efficiently generate a diverse, high-quality corpus of challenging multimodal scenarios and associated human preference judgments, which will reveal significant, systematic failure cases in state-of-the-art VLMs that are missed by static benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage iterative crowdsourcing pipeline. First, participants generate open-ended, context-rich image-text scenarios designed to probe specific capabilities (e.g., spatial reasoning, cultural knowledge). Second, other participants provide pairwise preference judgments on model outputs for these scenarios, creating a dynamic reward signal. We will leverage the researcher's expertise in CAT for adaptive visual tokenization to present complex images efficiently and use insights from Multimodal RewardBench to structure the human preference collection. The collected data will train a 'critic' model to score VLM outputs, creating an automated but human-grounded evaluation metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce (1) a novel, open-source dataset of dynamically generated, challenging VLM evaluation scenarios with human preference labels, and (2) a new evaluation paradigm and automated metric that directly reflects human judgment of failure cases. It will provide a more robust, adaptive, and realistic measure of VLM performance and safety.
proposals.novelty: While crowdsourcing is used for dataset creation, its application for the continuous, adversarial discovery of evaluation tasks and failure metrics—forming a living benchmark—is novel. This moves beyond static benchmarks and automated OOD detectors by directly embedding human creativity and judgment into the evaluation loop.

proposals.title: Component-Level Holistic Evaluation (CLEAR): Diagnosing Abstraction and Reward in Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of large multimodal models focuses on final output quality, treating the model as a black box. This obscures understanding of how specific components (e.g., vision encoders, adapter layers, reward heads) contribute to capabilities and failures, particularly for abstract reasoning and alignment. A holistic, component-level diagnostic is needed to guide efficient model design and targeted improvements in robustness and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic, targeted probing of intermediate representations and specific model components will reveal distinct, quantifiable failure modes in abstraction and reward modeling that are not apparent from end-to-end evaluation alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the holistic evaluation framework from Multimodal RewardBench to intermediate model stages. Using a suite of diagnostic datasets spanning abstraction levels (from concrete object recognition to abstract reasoning), we will apply probing classifiers and representation similarity analysis to layers of vision encoders (e.g., CLIP, DINOv2) and multimodal adapters. For reward models, we will adapt methods from Out-of-Distribution detection to analyze the sensitivity of reward heads to different input features. Training data influence will be assessed via data attribution techniques on subsets defined by abstraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel diagnostic toolkit for model developers to pinpoint weak components, leading to more efficient architecture refinement. It will produce new insights into how abstraction is built across modalities and how reward signals propagate, contributing to more interpretable and reliable multimodal systems.
proposals.novelty: While holistic evaluation exists for outputs, applying it systematically to internal components and training data subsets for multimodal models is novel. This moves beyond diagnosing 'what' a model gets wrong to explain 'where' and 'why' in the model's architecture the failure occurs.

ideas.title: Expand evaluation to more languages and modalities
ideas.description: Extend the holistic evaluation framework beyond English text to include multilingual and multimodal models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Use cross-attention from multimodal models
ideas.description: Adapt cross-attention mechanisms from vision-language models for text-graph representation alignment.

ideas.title: Scale up model with more medical data
ideas.description: Pre-train a much larger model on a massive, curated corpus of multimodal medical data.

ideas.title: Improve cross-modal understanding benchmarks
ideas.description: Develop new benchmarks to better evaluate the cross-modal reasoning capabilities of unified models like Transfusion.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transfusion: Predict the Next Token and Diffuse Images with One Multi-Modal Model

ideas.title: Challenge need for large labeled source data
ideas.description: Achieve robustness with limited or no labeled data from the source training distribution.
ideas.sourceTitle: WILDS: A Benchmark of in-the-Wild Distribution Shifts

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Diagnostic Probes: Adapting Cognitive Psychology's Process Dissociation to AI Benchmarks
ideas.description: This research adapts the Process Dissociation Procedure from cognitive psychology to develop new diagnostic benchmarks for cross-modal AI models like Transfusion. The analogous solution from memory research uses clever task design to isolate automatic and controlled cognitive processes within a single paradigm. Adapted to AI evaluation, this involves creating benchmark tasks with paired 'inclusion' and 'exclusion' conditions. For example, an inclusion task might require answering a question by fusing complementary clues from an image and a caption, while the parallel exclusion task might require answering using only the image while ignoring contradictory information planted in the caption. By analyzing a model's performance profile across these conditions, the benchmark can quantitatively estimate the extent to which the model relies on superficial, modality-specific associations versus performing deep, integrative cross-modal reasoning, providing a more nuanced evaluation than current accuracy-based benchmarks.

ideas.title: Scale the Modality-Invariant Bottleneck, Not Just Modality Count
ideas.description: This approach scales multimodal integration by prioritizing the learning of a unified, abstract representation that is invariant to the specific input modality. The core research direction is to develop training objectives and architectures that force diverse sensory inputs (vision, audio, text, etc.) to map onto a shared latent space capturing their underlying conceptual content, stripping away modality-specific details. Scaling efforts focus on increasing the breadth of modalities this bottleneck can successfully absorb and the richness of the abstract concepts it can represent, ensuring that adding a new modality strengthens the system's core understanding rather than complicating its architecture. The result is a model whose capability scales with the number of integrated modalities in a coherent, composable way, avoiding the fragmentation of 'modality-specific experts'.

ideas.title: Scale across modalities: add vision or audio
ideas.description: Extend the generalist evaluation to multimodal tasks beyond pure language.
ideas.sourceTitle: Is ChatGPT a General-Purpose Natural Language Processing Task Solver?

ideas.title: Scale down to few-shot or zero-shot learning
ideas.description: Adapt the model to perform well with minimal task-specific training examples.
ideas.sourceTitle: GreaseLM: Graph REASoning Enhanced Language Models for Question Answering

ideas.title: Efficient Multi-Modal Architectures for Capability Emergence at Reduced Scale
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the brute-force scaling paradigm for achieving advanced multi-modal capabilities can be circumvented by co-designing highly efficient training methods with novel, data-efficient architectures. The core hypothesis is that the compositional and complementary nature of information across modalities (text, image, audio, video) provides a rich, structured learning signal that, if processed by architectures with built-in cross-modal efficiency (e.g., sparse mixture-of-experts, modality-specific token compression), can trigger emergent understanding and generation at a significantly reduced parameter and compute budget. The goal is to derive a new set of scaling laws and architectural principles that define the minimal efficient scale for multi-modal capability emergence, moving beyond mere cost reduction to a fundamental re-evaluation of how scale interacts with data structure.

ideas.title: Adversarial Task Priors: Hybrid Pretraining for Targeted Capability with Certified Robustness
ideas.description: This research investigates a hybrid pretraining paradigm designed to capture the efficiency benefits of task-informed data while actively defending against the robustness collapse inherent in narrow pretraining. The core method involves a two-stream pretraining process where one stream uses curated data for the target task, and a parallel adversarial stream generates or retrieves examples that challenge the model's emerging task priors. The system is optimized under a meta-learning objective that balances high in-distribution accuracy with certified performance guarantees on a dynamic robustness benchmark, creating models that are both highly capable on the intended task and systematically resistant to distributional shift and adversarial manipulation.

ideas.title: Apply REPLUG to diverse downstream tasks
ideas.description: Extend REPLUG's evaluation beyond language modeling and MMLU to tasks like open-domain QA and dialogue.
ideas.sourceTitle: REPLUG: Retrieval-Augmented Black-Box Language Models

ideas.title: Universal Tokenization via Sensory Transduction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that different modalities require fundamentally different, handcrafted encoding architectures (e.g., CNNs for vision, spectrograms for audio, tokenizers for text). Drawing an analogy from neurobiology, where diverse sensory inputs are transduced into a common electrochemical language (action potentials) for homogeneous neural processing, we propose training a model with universal sensory transduction. The solution involves creating lightweight, learnable input 'transducers' for each modality. These are not full encoding stacks but minimal adaptive interfaces that project raw or minimally pre-processed inputs into the same latent token space expected by a single, homogeneous processing core (e.g., a transformer). All parameters—both in the modality-specific transducers and the shared core—are trained jointly end-to-end. The goal is to discover a truly unified representation space and processing pathway, potentially improving cross-modal alignment and efficiency by forcing the model to find the common computational primitives underlying all data types.

ideas.title: Adopt test-time training for adaptation
ideas.description: Apply test-time training methods to adapt the model using few-shot examples at inference.
ideas.sourceTitle: Med-Flamingo: a Multimodal Medical Few-shot Learner

ideas.title: Scaling Laws for Homogeneous Multimodal Architectures
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to scale a truly homogeneous multimodal transformer, where all input modalities (text, image, audio, video) are projected into a shared embedding space using a single, universal linear layer and processed by an identical stack of transformer layers. The core experiment is to train a series of these models, scaling from ~1B to over 100B parameters, on a massive, aligned dataset of all modalities, to derive new scaling laws specific to this simplified architecture. The goal is to determine if this forced parameter sharing accelerates the emergence of unified representations and cross-modal reasoning abilities compared to heterogeneous models, and whether it leads to more favorable compute-optimal scaling curves.

ideas.title: Challenge reliance on paired image-text data
ideas.description: Develop methods that learn from unpaired or weakly labeled medical images and text.
ideas.sourceTitle: Med-Flamingo: a Multimodal Medical Few-shot Learner

ideas.title: Create a low-resource few-shot benchmark
ideas.description: Define a task where models must adapt to a new domain with only a few example dialogues.
ideas.sourceTitle: CoSQL: A Conversational Text-to-SQL Challenge Towards Cross-Domain Natural Language Interfaces to Databases

ideas.title: Multimodal Integration via Cross-Modal Scaffolding
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from developmental biology, where scaling a complex system like an organ requires an integrative scaffold, not just more cells. The analogous problem of scaling AI models is solved by shifting focus from parameter count to multimodal integration depth. We propose to design and scale a 'cross-modal scaffolding' architecture—a dynamic, sparse computational framework that explicitly orchestrates the deep integration of diverse modalities (text, image, audio, etc.). Scaling involves enhancing this scaffold's complexity and connectivity to guide richer, more structured cross-modal interactions, ensuring that model growth directly translates to improved multimodal reasoning and synthesis, not just increased capacity within isolated modal streams.

ideas.title: Challenge generative-only VQA evaluation
ideas.description: Investigate if discriminative or retrieval-based approaches are better for certain medical VQA tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Med-Flamingo: a Multimodal Medical Few-shot Learner

ideas.title: Treat images as discrete tokens from the start
ideas.description: Challenge the continuous diffusion approach by forcing images into a discrete latent space for unified language modeling.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transfusion: Predict the Next Token and Diffuse Images with One Multi-Modal Model

ideas.title: Architecture-Coordinated Modality-Specific Processing
ideas.description: This research investigates a co-design of a non-transformer sequence model backbone (e.g., based on State Space Models or other efficient architectures) with deep, modality-specific neural encoders and decoders. The core synergy is that the architectural properties of the backbone—such as its handling of long-range dependencies, recurrence, or convolutional structures—are used to dictate the design and internal mechanisms of the modality-specific networks, moving far beyond simple linear layers. Conversely, the rich, structured outputs from these advanced encoders are tailored to be optimally processed by the specific strengths and dynamics of the chosen backbone. The goal is to achieve a more cohesive and parameter-efficient model that excels at the joint next-token prediction and diffusion tasks by having each component intrinsically aligned with the other's computational philosophy.

ideas.title: Hypernetwork-Assembled Meta-Loss Framework for Multi-Modal Learning
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework that abandons the pursuit of a single monolithic loss in favor of a unified, compositional system for constructing tailored objectives. A small set of interpretable, foundational loss primitives is defined, each targeting a core statistical property like distribution matching or consistency. A conditioning hypernetwork, informed by data modality descriptors and task embeddings, dynamically assembles and weights these primitives into a coherent, problem-specific composite loss. This approach provides the flexibility of specialized losses while maintaining a unified, learnable parameter space that can transfer insights across modalities and tasks.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Training Data Forensics via Cross-Modal Inversion
ideas.description: This research develops methods to apply inverse problem techniques, like training data distillation and bias inference, to unified multi-modal models (e.g., Transfusion architectures extended to audio, video, and 3D data). The core innovation is cross-modal inversion: using a model's learned representations in one modality (e.g., text) to infer statistical properties, missing data patterns, or potential biases in the training data of a different, co-trained modality (e.g., video or audio). This creates a powerful forensic tool for auditing large-scale multi-modal training pipelines, allowing researchers to diagnose dataset imbalances, uncover hidden cross-modal correlations in the training set, and verify data provenance without direct access to the original, often proprietary, datasets.

ideas.title: Modular, Pluggable Encoder-Decoder Units for Post-Deployment Model Adaptation
ideas.description: This research proposes a new adaptation paradigm where a foundational model is equipped with a standardized interface for attaching external, sophisticated modality-specific encoder-decoder units. Instead of fine-tuning the entire model for a new data type (e.g., high-resolution spectral data, 3D point clouds), one designs a compact, specialized module that performs high-fidelity compression and reconstruction into the model's existing latent space. This allows the core model's knowledge to remain intact and stable, while adaptation and performance gains are achieved by composing and upgrading these modular, plug-and-play processing units, effectively decoupling modality expertise from core reasoning.

ideas.title: Edge-Universal Foundation Models via a Unified Multi-Modal Loss
ideas.description: This research proposes the co-design of a unified, information-theoretic loss function and a radically efficient model architecture to create a single foundation model deployable on edge hardware. The core innovation is a loss that generalizes across discrete tokens and continuous signals, allowing the model to learn a shared, compressed representation for diverse data modalities. By training this single model from scratch with the unified objective, we achieve a drastic reduction in parameters and computational footprint compared to stitching together separate specialized models. The result is a versatile, edge-native AI capable of on-device multi-modal reasoning and generation without reliance on cloud APIs.

ideas.title: Multimodal Stress Testing: Scaling Integration to Expose and Characterize Cross-Modal Failure Modes
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a co-design of scaled multimodal architectures and novel evaluation suites. Instead of scaling for general capability, the focus is on architecting models to integrate an increasing number of modalities (e.g., audio, video, tactile, physiological signals) in deliberately challenging ways to surface failure points. Concurrently, it develops rigorous 'multimodal stress tests'—benchmarks that systematically probe cross-modal alignment, consistency, and reasoning under distribution shifts or adversarial perturbations across modalities. The core synergy is that the evaluation framework actively informs how to scale integration (e.g., which fusion mechanisms are most brittle), while the scaled, complex models provide the necessary substrate to discover higher-order, emergent failures that simpler models cannot exhibit.

ideas.title: Discrete Latent Auditing and Repair for Inherited Visual Model Defects
ideas.description: This research proposes a method to audit and mitigate inherited defects from large-scale visual generative models by enforcing a discrete, interpretable latent token space from the start. By treating images as sequences of discrete tokens, core flaws—such as biases, structural artifacts, or safety violations—become traceable as malformed or skewed token distributions. This allows for the development of targeted 'repair' techniques, like token distribution editing or constrained decoding, to correct these defects at the source before they propagate to downstream models. The result is a unified language-model-like framework for visual generation that inherently supports the identification and remediation of homogenized model failures.

ideas.title: Architectural Fingerprinting: Inferring Training Data Characteristics via Non-Transformer Backbone Probing
ideas.description: This research investigates whether core characteristics of a model's training data can be more effectively distilled or inferred by analyzing models built on non-transformer backbone architectures (e.g., state-space models, recurrent networks) trained on combined next-token and diffusion objectives. By comparing the data fingerprints extracted from these diverse architectures against those from standard transformers, we can disentangle which inferred data properties are fundamental to the learning tasks and which are artifacts of the transformer's specific inductive biases. This approach provides a new lens for model auditing and safety, allowing researchers to attribute memorization, bias, and data leakage risks to specific architectural choices rather than treating the model as a black box. The outcome is a methodology for architectural-aware data distillation that could inform safer, more transparent model design.

ideas.title: Design a Shared Latent Space with Cross-Modal Reconstruction Loss
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience, where the brain prioritizes integrated multisensory percepts over perfect unimodal fidelity, this idea inverts the standard AI design goal. The analogous solution from biology is a neural architecture wired for cross-modal integration. Adapted to machine learning, the research idea is to design a model with a shared latent space explicitly optimized by a primary cross-modal reconstruction loss (e.g., image-from-text). Uni-modal encoders become auxiliary components regularized by this space, meaning the architecture is fundamentally designed for cross-modal tasks, potentially accepting a decrease in standalone performance on unimodal benchmarks as a necessary trade-off for superior integrative ability.

ideas.title: Scaling Laws for Data Transparency: Inferring Training Data Properties from Ultra-Large Transfusion Models
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the two-way relationship between extreme model scale and training data inference. By pretraining a series of Transfusion models scaling from 7B to hundreds of billions of parameters, we will apply and develop new methods to distill statistical properties, compositional biases, and potential memorization signatures from the models. Crucially, we will analyze how the effectiveness and nature of this data inference change as a function of model scale, testing whether scaling laws exist for data transparency and extractability. The synergy provides a principled, scaling-aware methodology to audit and characterize the data footprints of massive models, which is impossible by studying scale or data inference in isolation.

ideas.title: Modality-Coordinated Encoding with Shared Alignment for Unified Multimodal Latent Spaces
ideas.description: This research designs a framework where sophisticated, modality-specific encoders are developed not in isolation, but under a coordination constraint. Each encoder feeds into a small, shared alignment network that projects features into a unified latent space. A central coordination module applies contrastive and reconstruction-based losses to maximize both the fidelity of individual modality compression and the semantic alignment across modalities. This approach ensures that gains in single-modality quality directly contribute to, rather than hinder, robust cross-modal understanding and generation.

ideas.title: Incorporate retrieval-augmented generation for multi-modal contexts
ideas.description: Augment the Transfusion model with a retrieval mechanism to access external multi-modal memory during generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transfusion: Predict the Next Token and Diffuse Images with One Multi-Modal Model

ideas.title: Adaptive Curriculum Learning for Multi-Modal Training via Data Assimilation
ideas.description: This research adapts the core solution from Numerical Weather Prediction—data assimilation—to optimize training on massive mixed-modality datasets. In NWP, diverse, sparse observational data is efficiently integrated into a physical model by weighting sources based on uncertainty and forecast relevance. Adapted to machine learning, we propose developing a dynamic, adaptive curriculum learning framework. A meta-controller would continuously assess the training state ('model forecast') and the heterogeneous stream of multi-modal tokens ('observations'). It would then schedule and weight the assimilation of token batches from different modalities (text, image, audio patches) based on real-time metrics of their informativeness and the model's current learning gaps. This selective, prioritized integration aims to reduce redundant computation and accelerate convergence by maximizing the training signal per processed token, significantly cutting the computational cost of training on long, mixed-modality sequences.

ideas.title: Scale to streaming data with continuous evaluation

ideas.title: Applying Pharmaceutical Drug Discovery Principles to Data Curation for Model Safety
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that model capability and safety scale primarily with dataset size. Drawing an analogy from pharmaceutical drug discovery, where the field moved from brute-force high-throughput screening to rational design using curated, high-quality compound libraries, we propose a parallel shift in AI data strategy. The analogous solution involved prioritizing chemical purity, known biological relevance, and structural diversity over sheer quantity to improve drug candidate efficacy and safety profiles. Adapted to machine learning, this suggests systematically constructing a meticulously curated, high-quality dataset where each data point is validated for factual accuracy, ethical alignment, lack of toxic content, and pedagogical utility for teaching desired model behaviors. The core research question becomes: Can such a rationally designed, quality-weighted data library significantly reduce the total scale of data required to train a capable model, while simultaneously and measurably improving its safety and alignment properties, as assessed by rigorous red-teaming and safety benchmarks? This approach inverts the prevailing scale-centric paradigm by testing if data quality can act as a multiplier, making smaller-scale training both feasible and safer.

ideas.title: Vaccine-Inspired Targeted Pretraining: Using Controlled Exposure to Build Robustness
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from immunology, this idea inverts the assumption of task-agnostic pretraining by proposing a vaccine-inspired, targeted pretraining paradigm. In vaccinology, controlled exposure to specific antigens trains the immune system more effectively and safely than broad exposure to all pathogens. The analogous solution is adapted by designing a model 'vaccination' phase using curated datasets that represent specific, known failure modes, adversarial examples, or critical task domains. The research would investigate whether this targeted, task-informed 'priming' yields models with better performance on the targeted tasks and more robust, intrinsic safety characteristics compared to models born from purely broad, undifferentiated data pretraining.

ideas.title: Continuously update HELM with new models and scenarios
ideas.description: Maintain HELM as a living benchmark by adding new models, scenarios, and metrics over time.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Extend Transfusion via a Universal Signal Encoder, inspired by Sensor Fusion
ideas.description: This idea proposes extending the Transfusion recipe to audio, video, and 3D data by drawing an analogy from sensor fusion in robotics. In robotics, heterogeneous sensor streams are fused by converting them into a common intermediate representation (like an occupancy grid) for unified processing. The adapted solution is to design a trainable 'Universal Signal Encoder' that projects these new modalities into the same latent embedding space used for text and images in Transfusion. This encoder would be integrated and co-trained within the existing framework using alignment objectives, thereby allowing the model to process and generate across a truly unified set of modalities by abstracting their raw signals into a consistent representational format.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate images from text
ideas.description: Develop a model that generates medical images or diagrams from textual clinical descriptions.

ideas.sourceTitle: Med-Flamingo: a Multimodal Medical Few-shot Learner

ideas.title: A Thermodynamic Free Energy Objective for Generative Modeling
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that discrete (e.g., language modeling) and continuous (e.g., diffusion) data generation require fundamentally separate loss functions. Drawing an analogy from statistical physics, where the free energy potential unifies the description of systems with both discrete and continuous degrees of freedom under a single minimization principle, we propose to design a unified generative objective. The analogous solution uses free energy, which combines an energy term and an entropy term. Adapted to machine learning, we design a single, parameterized objective comprising a data fidelity/distortion component and a distributional diversity/entropy component. This 'machine learning free energy' can be specialized via its balancing parameter (acting like a temperature) and choice of divergence to recover or interpolate between cross-entropy (LM) and variational lower bound/score matching (diffusion) losses, providing a common framework for training hybrid or general-purpose generative models.

ideas.title: Scale down models to match human problem-solving data
ideas.description: Train small models on human reasoning traces from BIG-bench tasks.

ideas.title: Operationalizing and Scaling Safety-Centric Data Quality
ideas.description: This research formally defines and measures 'safety-relevant data quality' through automated, quantifiable proxies rather than subjective human judgment. It systematically tests scaling laws along axes of both data quantity and these quality metrics to identify their independent and interactive effects on model safety and capabilities. The final validation involves rigorous stress-testing on novel, held-out adversarial prompts and safety benchmarks to ensure that improvements from the quality metrics generalize robustly beyond the training distribution.

ideas.title: Apply contrastive learning for alignment

ideas.title: Differentially Private Transfusion for Sensitive Multi-Modal Data
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the Transfusion framework for unified multi-modal training with rigorous differential privacy (DP) techniques. The core innovation is developing and scaling DP-SGD and related mechanisms for the unique computational and representational challenges of training with fused audio, video, and 3D data streams. The synergy creates a pathway to train powerful, unified models on corpora containing sensitive personal or proprietary information—such as medical records, confidential meetings, or copyrighted 3D assets—that were previously off-limits. This would provide formal privacy guarantees against memorization and membership inference attacks while still achieving the representational benefits of cross-modal learning.

ideas.title: Incorporate retrieval-augmented generation for SKG

ideas.title: Ecological Resilience-Inspired Training for Efficient Multi-Modal Learning
ideas.description: This research investigates how principles of ecological resilience—such as functional redundancy, modularity, and adaptive cycles—can be translated into novel training algorithms and architectures for large multi-modal models. Instead of viewing training efficiency as a static optimization problem, we model the training process and the resulting model as an adaptive ecosystem, where computational resources (energy) and data streams (nutrients) are allocated to promote robust, generalizable learning with minimal catastrophic forgetting. The goal is to develop training regimes that are not only computationally cheaper but also yield models that are inherently more adaptable to new tasks or data modalities, much like a resilient ecosystem can maintain function under stress.

ideas.title: Use a non-transformer backbone architecture
ideas.description: Investigate if other sequence model architectures can effectively learn the combined next-token and diffusion tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Transfusion: Predict the Next Token and Diffuse Images with One Multi-Modal Model

ideas.title: Neuroscience-Inspired Interpretability for Data Quality Auditing and Curation
ideas.description: This research direction applies computational neuroscience techniques—such as representational similarity analysis, lesion studies, and single-neuron/feature visualization—to systematically audit and define 'data quality' for foundation model training. Instead of using interpretability only to understand a finished model, these methods are used to analyze model internals *during* training on different data mixtures, identifying specific data properties that lead to robust, generalizable, and safe internal representations. The insights directly guide the curation of high-quality datasets, creating a feedback loop where improved data quality reduces the scale needed for performance while simultaneously producing models whose internal mechanisms are more interpretable and aligned by design.

ideas.title: Hypothesis-Driven Benchmark of Non-Transformer Architectures for Unified Generative Modeling
ideas.description: This research systematically evaluates specific non-transformer sequence models for combined next-token and diffusion tasks by testing clear architectural hypotheses. Candidate models are selected based on theoretical properties like linear-time sequence modeling or inherent statefulness, which may offer efficiency or stability advantages for the dual objective. A controlled experimental framework trains each candidate and a transformer baseline from scratch on identical datasets and compute budgets, measuring performance on both tasks and scaling behavior. The outcome is a principled assessment of whether and which alternative architectures can surpass transformers in this unified setting, moving beyond anecdotal exploration.

ideas.title: Immunological Adversarial Training for Foundation Models
ideas.description: Drawing from immunology and vaccinology, this approach adapts the concept of acquired immunity to AI safety. Just as vaccines expose the immune system to harmless versions of pathogens to build defensive memory, we propose exposing foundation models to carefully engineered, non-damaging adversarial inputs and failure scenarios during training. A separate ensemble of 'immune' detector models is trained not just to recognize these specific failures, but to develop a generalized 'recognition capability' for failure patterns. This creates a scalable detection system that can identify novel failures by their structural resemblance to known vaccine-exposed failure modes, enabling proactive identification of model weaknesses before deployment, much like immune memory provides protection against future infections.

ideas.title: Neural Adaptation via Epigenetic-Inspired Control Layers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that transfer learning must occur via fine-tuning the core model parameters. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and epigenetics, where organisms use regulatory mechanisms to adapt gene expression without changing DNA, we propose a novel adaptation method for neural networks. The analogous solution involves rapid, reversible epigenetic controls. Adapted to machine learning, this translates to researching lightweight, trainable 'epigenetic' control layers—such as dynamic activation modulators or learned attention routers—that are appended to a frozen pre-trained model. These layers would learn to reconfigure the information flow and feature emphasis of the foundational model for new tasks, providing a modular, parameter-efficient, and potentially more interpretable path to adaptation beyond wholesale fine-tuning.

ideas.title: Dynamic Adversarial Evaluation Framework with Automated Red-Teaming
ideas.description: This research designs an evaluation ecosystem where benchmarks are not static datasets but active, adaptive systems. Core to the framework is a suite of automated red-team agents that employ diverse strategies—from gradient-based attacks to LLM-driven prompt generation and simulation-based edge-case discovery—to probe a model's weaknesses in real-time. The system continuously updates challenge suites based on discovered failures, creating a moving target that tests a model's resilience to novel threats. This shifts evaluation from a passive audit of known issues to a proactive stress test of general robustness and adaptive failure resistance.

ideas.title: Structured Adaptation Pathways: A Unified Framework for Model Adaptation Beyond Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This research develops a principled framework that unifies diverse adaptation methods—including fine-tuning, modular extensions (e.g., adapters, side networks), and external reasoning systems—under a common theoretical and optimization-based umbrella. The framework formally defines a space of adaptation operators and provides mechanisms to dynamically compose them based on task specifications, data availability, and desired system attributes like parameter efficiency, knowledge isolation, or incremental learning capability. By doing so, it moves beyond simply cataloging alternatives to enabling systematic, goal-driven construction of hybrid adaptation strategies with clear performance and robustness guarantees, directly addressing the fragmentation and lack of cohesion in prior approaches.

ideas.title: Adapting Hierarchical Signal Processing for Modality-Specific Neural Encoders
ideas.description: This idea proposes to design more sophisticated modality-specific encoders by drawing an analogy from seismic imaging. In geophysics, raw sensor data is processed through a hierarchical, multi-resolution pipeline where simple initial filtering is followed by progressively more complex, domain-optimized techniques to extract and compress essential structural information. Adapted to multimodal AI, this suggests replacing initial linear projection layers with hierarchical, multi-stage encoders for each modality. For example, an image encoder might begin with a coarse color/normalization stage, then apply a wavelet-based network for multi-scale feature extraction, followed by a spatial attention mechanism. Similarly, an audio encoder could use a filterbank mimicking the cochlea, then temporal convolutional networks, and finally a spectral refinement stage. This structured, domain-aware approach aims to dramatically improve the compression and reconstruction quality of individual modalities before fusion, leading to better overall model performance.

ideas.title: Formally Verified Discrete Token Models for Provable Image Generation Safety
ideas.description: This research direction proposes building foundation models for image generation that operate natively on discrete visual tokens, creating a unified symbolic sequence space for images and text. By treating images as discrete code, the model's generation process becomes analogous to a program execution, making it amenable to formal verification techniques like model checking and theorem proving. This allows for the specification and automated verification of critical safety properties—such as the guaranteed non-generation of certain visual concepts or strict compositional adherence to prompts—before or during the sampling process. The result is a pathway to provably safe and controllable image synthesis, a capability beyond the reach of either continuous latent spaces or post-hoc content filtering alone.

ideas.title: Inverse Spectroscopy: Reconstructing Training Data Signatures via Model Perturbation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the inverse problem-solving approach of astrophysical spectroscopy to machine learning. In spectroscopy, a source's intrinsic properties are deduced from its observed light spectrum. Analogously, we propose to treat a trained model as the observable output 'spectrum' generated by the 'source' of its training data. The core method involves developing 'inverse spectroscopic' techniques: by applying controlled perturbations to model inputs or probing internal activations with specific queries, we analyze the resulting shifts in the model's output patterns (e.g., confidence distributions, feature embeddings, loss landscapes). These shifts act as 'absorption/emission lines' that encode fingerprints of the training data's composition. The goal is to systematize this perturbation-analysis framework to distill core dataset characteristics—such as label distributions, the presence of underrepresented groups, or statistical biases—directly from the final, deployed model parameters, providing a tool for auditing and understanding model provenance.

ideas.title: Adapting Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) for AI Model Evaluation
ideas.description: This research adapts the Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) methodology from Systems and Reliability Engineering to the evaluation of AI models. The analogous solution is a structured, proactive risk assessment technique used to identify potential failure points in complex systems, score them by Severity, Occurrence, and Detectability, and prioritize mitigation efforts. The adapted idea is to create a new AI evaluation benchmark framework that systematically catalogs model failure modes across diverse inputs and conditions. Researchers would define failure modes (e.g., vulnerability to specific spatial reasoning prompts or stylistic paraphrases), then score and rank them using adapted FMEA criteria. This produces a quantifiable 'risk profile' for a model, moving beyond aggregate accuracy to characterize which failures are most critical and likely, thereby directing the development of more targeted robustness tests and stress-test benchmarks.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Impact Assessment Frameworks for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes the development of interdisciplinary frameworks that use cross-modal task performance as the central, measurable proxy for a foundation model's capacity for real-world, context-aware reasoning—a prerequisite for responsible societal integration. By prioritizing cross-modal over uni-modal optimization during design, the technical system is inherently aligned with the complex, multi-faceted nature of societal problems, which rarely exist within a single data modality. The fused framework then embeds this technical design choice into collaborative legal and ethical analysis, creating concrete evaluation protocols where cross-modal capabilities are assessed for their implications on fairness, accountability, and transparency across diverse application domains. This synergy ensures that technical development and impact assessment are co-designed around the same core competency, leading to models whose architectures are not only more robust but also more legible and auditable from a societal standpoint.

ideas.title: Scaling Transfusion Models via Astrophysical Simulation Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'zoom-in' hierarchical simulation technique from computational astrophysics to the challenge of scaling Transfusion models beyond 7B parameters. In astrophysics, simulating galaxy formation across cosmic scales is achieved by first running low-resolution simulations of the large-scale universe to identify regions of interest, then applying immense computational resources to simulate only those critical regions at high resolution. Analogously, we propose a two-phase methodology: first, conduct a systematic series of pretraining runs at intermediate scales (1B to ~20B parameters) to empirically derive refined scaling laws for Transfusion architectures, specifically identifying the data mixtures, architectural tweaks, and task families where performance scales most favorably. Second, use these laws as a guide to 'zoom in' by concentrating the vast compute required for a 100B+ parameter model on pretraining with the optimally scaled data and tasks, thereby efficiently navigating the high-dimensional scaling landscape and mitigating the extreme risk and cost of naive large-scale pretraining.
